events.text: The patient is a 52-year-old female named Margaret Osei-Bonsu. She is a never-smoker with no alcohol use history, of West African descent, works as a high school music teacher. She has a history of monogamous partnership and remote HPV exposure. She has no significant past medical history other than well-controlled hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. She has no family history of head and neck cancers.

At age 52 years, the patient was diagnosed with stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition p16-positive oropharyngeal group: stage I by HPV-positive staging criteria) squamous cell carcinoma of the base of tongue (ICD-O site C01.9, histology code 8070/3: squamous cell carcinoma, NOS). The tumor measured 3.2 cm at the base of tongue with a single ipsilateral level II lymph node measuring 2.1 cm, no distant metastases. The tumor was p16 positive (surrogate for HPV), confirming HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. Relevant biomarkers: p16 IHC strongly positive (3+, >75% of cells), HPV in situ hybridization positive for high-risk HPV (subtype 16), EBER negative. PD-L1 CPS score: 12. Tumor confirmation: positive histology via direct laryngoscopy biopsy. Smoking history: never smoker (0 pack-years). ECOG performance status: 0. The patient initially presented with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Oropharyngoscopy revealed an exophytic, friable mass at the right base of tongue with right level II lymphadenopathy.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of the right base of tongue mass. Pathology showed invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated (grade 2), arising from the base of tongue mucosa with keratinization and intercellular bridges. Immunohistochemistry: p16 diffusely and strongly positive (block-like nuclear and cytoplasmic staining in >75% of tumor cells), consistent with transcriptionally active HPV infection. CK5/6 positive, p63 positive, CK7 negative. Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 65%. HPV RNA ISH performed concurrently confirmed high-risk HPV type 16 RNA expression in tumor cells. Pathologic margins: not applicable (biopsy). Fine needle aspiration of right level II lymph node: positive for metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, p16 positive.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent an MRI of the neck with and without contrast for staging. MRI demonstrated a 3.2 x 2.8 cm enhancing mass centered at the right base of tongue with extension toward the right tonsillar fossa but without extension to the vallecula or pre-epiglottic space. There was a single right level IIA lymph node measuring 2.1 x 1.8 cm with internal necrosis and peripheral enhancement, consistent with metastatic adenopathy. No bilateral or contralateral adenopathy identified. No evidence of retropharyngeal lymphadenopathy. No bony involvement of the mandible or skull base. No perineural invasion identified on imaging. Overall impression: T2N1M0, stage III (AJCC 8th ed. conventional), stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent PET-CT (18F-FDG) for staging. PET-CT confirmed an FDG-avid mass at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 14.2) with a single FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node (SUVmax 9.8, measuring 2.1 cm). No contralateral cervical adenopathy. No mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular nodal disease. No distant metastases. Lungs: clear, no nodules. Liver, spleen: unremarkable. Impression: FDG-avid right base of tongue primary with single right-sided regional nodal metastasis. Consistent with stage III (T2N1M0) oropharyngeal SCC, favorable HPV-positive subtype.

At age 52 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing (NGS) performed on tumor biopsy tissue from the right base of tongue using a comprehensive solid tumor panel (523-gene panel). Results: PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (allele frequency 18%, pathogenic, actionable); TERT promoter C228T mutation (allele frequency 22%, pathogenic); FAT1 p.Q2401* nonsense mutation (allele frequency 15%, likely pathogenic); EP300 p.R1627Q missense mutation (allele frequency 11%, variant of uncertain significance); NOTCH1 p.A465T missense mutation (allele frequency 9%, variant of uncertain significance). Copy number alterations: CCND1 amplification (6.4-fold), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, EGFR copy number gain (low-level, 3.1 copies). No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, or TP53 mutations detected. No actionable fusions (ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, FGFR2/3 all negative). MSI status: microsatellite stable (MSS). TMB: 6.2 mutations/Mb (low). PD-L1 (SP142): CPS 12. No EGFR exon 19/21 activating mutations. HPV integration detected. Germline testing recommended given BRCA2 variant of unknown significance identified (p.D2723H, AF 48%, likely germline). Note: EGFR amplification and CCND1 amplification are co-occurring alterations commonly seen in HPV-positive OPSCC; the absence of TP53 mutation is consistent with HPV-driven oncogenesis.

At age 52 years, the patient was seen in multidisciplinary tumor board (head and neck oncology, radiation oncology, speech-language pathology, dietetics, social work). The primary recommendation was definitive concurrent chemoradiation with cisplatin-based chemotherapy and intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT), given T2N1M0 HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC in a never-smoker. Surgical resection (transoral robotic surgery, TORS) was discussed but the tumor board recommended against upfront surgery given the nodal involvement and the established efficacy of definitive CRT in this setting. De-escalation trials (ECOG 3311, NRG HN002) were discussed but the patient was not enrolled. The patient was counseled extensively on acute toxicities including mucositis, xerostomia, dysphagia, and the need for potential nutritional support. Baseline FEES (functional endoscopic evaluation of swallowing): mild pharyngeal phase delay, no aspiration on thin liquids. Baseline audiogram: normal bilateral hearing thresholds. Baseline creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, GFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2. Dental evaluation completed: no extractions required, dental clearance obtained. PEG tube placement discussed prophylactically given concern for nutritional compromise during treatment. Patient consented to treatment. Cancer present.

At age 52 years, the patient underwent percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) tube placement by interventional gastroenterology, in anticipation of nutritional compromise during concurrent chemoradiation. The procedure was uncomplicated. The patient was counseled by dietetics on PEG tube management, caloric requirements (~2200 kcal/day during treatment), and transitional feeding strategies. The plan was for PEG use on an as-needed basis with preference for continued oral intake as tolerated.

At age 52 years, the patient began definitive intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. Treatment plan: high-risk CTV (primary tumor + right level IIA node) received 70 Gy in 35 fractions (2.0 Gy/fraction); intermediate-risk CTV (right neck levels II-IV, bilateral retropharyngeal nodes) received 63 Gy in 35 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction); low-risk CTV (bilateral neck levels II-IV elective) received 56 Gy in 35 fractions (1.6 Gy/fraction). Sparing structures: mean dose to contralateral parotid 22 Gy (goal <26 Gy achieved), mean spinal cord max <45 Gy, mandible max <70 Gy. Treatment delivered 5 days per week over 7 weeks. Setup: thermoplastic immobilization mask, daily CBCT image guidance. IMRT delivered using VMAT (volumetric modulated arc therapy) technique with 2 arcs. Total treatment duration: 7 weeks. No treatment interruptions. Concurrent with weekly cisplatin chemotherapy.

At age 52 years, the patient received concurrent cisplatin chemotherapy administered weekly during IMRT at a dose of 40 mg/m2 IV (body surface area 1.72 m2, dose 69 mg IV weekly) with standard pre-hydration (1 L normal saline over 1 hour pre-cisplatin) and post-hydration (1 L normal saline over 2 hours post-cisplatin), ondansetron 24 mg IV, dexamethasone 12 mg IV, and magnesium sulfate 2 g IV as antiemetic prophylaxis. Amifostine was not used. The patient received 6 of 7 planned weekly cycles; week 5 dose was held due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine rise to 1.3 mg/dL) and grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 88 x10^9/L). Cumulative cisplatin dose delivered: 414 mg/m2 (83% of planned dose). This was considered adequate systemic therapy.

At age 52 years, during week 4 of concurrent chemoradiation, the patient developed grade 3 oral mucositis (confluent pseudomembranous mucositis involving the base of tongue, bilateral oral mucosa, and oropharynx) per CTCAE v5.0 criteria. She reported severe odynophagia (pain score 9/10), inability to swallow thin liquids, and significant weight loss of 6 kg (8% body weight). She was admitted to the hospital for 3 days for IV hydration, electrolyte repletion (magnesium 2.5 mg/dL, repleted with IV mag sulfate), and pain management (initiation of patient-controlled analgesia with morphine, transitioned to extended-release oxycodone with immediate-release oxycodone for breakthrough). PEG tube feeds were initiated at this time with goal rate of Vital 1.5 at 75 mL/hr for 16 hours per day (1800 kcal/day via tube). The patient was discharged with close outpatient follow-up and was able to complete the radiation treatment without further interruptions. Oral mucositis management included magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine/lidocaine/Maalox), oral cryotherapy, and dexamethasone oral rinse.

At age 52 years, concurrent with radiation treatment, the patient also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis (moist desquamation of right neck skin in radiation field), grade 2 dysgeusia (significant taste alteration), grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 1 fatigue. Dermatitis was managed with barrier cream (Mepitel film, aquaphor). Grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, baseline 0.8 mg/dL) developed during week 5 of treatment, necessitating a 1-week cisplatin hold and aggressive IV hydration. Creatinine returned to 1.0 mg/dL after hydration. No ototoxicity noted on monitoring audiogram performed after 4th cisplatin cycle. No peripheral neuropathy developed.

At age 52 years, immediately following completion of concurrent chemoradiation, the patient was seen in oncology and radiation follow-up. She had lost a total of 9 kg (12% body weight) during treatment and was fully dependent on PEG tube nutrition. Physical examination showed resolving mucositis (now grade 1-2, improving). Right neck palpation: previously palpable right level II node now no longer palpable. ECOG performance status: 2 (due to fatigue, pain, and dysphagia). She was continued on PEG tube feeds and initiated aggressive swallowing rehabilitation with speech-language pathology (daily exercises, thermal-tactile stimulation). Plan was for restaging imaging at 8-12 weeks post-treatment per NCCN guidelines. Cancer status: indeterminate (too early post-treatment for assessment).

At age 52 years, approximately 10 weeks after completion of definitive chemoradiation, the patient underwent restaging PET-CT. Results: Complete metabolic response. No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 2.1, consistent with post-treatment changes/physiologic activity). Previously FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node: now resolved, replaced by a 0.8 cm fibrotic-appearing node with no FDG uptake (SUVmax 1.8). No new areas of FDG-avid disease. No distant metastases. Impression: Complete metabolic response to concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive right base of tongue SCC. No evidence of residual or recurrent disease. Residual inflammatory changes in pharyngeal mucosa consistent with post-radiation changes.

At age 52 years, approximately 10 weeks after completion of chemoradiation, MRI of the neck with and without contrast was also obtained for anatomic correlation. MRI showed significant decrease in the right base of tongue mass: now measuring 0.9 x 0.7 cm with decreased enhancement and T2 signal, representing post-treatment fibrosis/scar vs. minimal residual tumor. Right level IIA lymph node now 0.8 cm with no internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy. No new lesions. Impression: Significant radiographic response, complete or near-complete, at the primary site and regional nodes. Consistent with PET-CT findings of complete metabolic response. No evidence of distant metastases.

At age 52 years (approximately 3 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in multidisciplinary follow-up. PET-CT and MRI confirmed complete metabolic response. Physical examination: oropharynx with post-radiation changes (mucositis resolved, now with visible fibrotic changes at right base of tongue), no palpable neck adenopathy. Swallowing rehabilitation progressing: patient able to tolerate pureed diet orally, PEG tube use decreased to approximately 50% of caloric needs. Weight stable at 67 kg (9 kg below pre-treatment weight). Creatinine normalized to 0.9 mg/dL. The tumor board discussed the need for adjuvant therapy: given complete response on PET-CT imaging and HPV-positive histology, no adjuvant systemic therapy or surgery was recommended. Surveillance schedule planned per NCCN HPV-positive OPSCC guidelines. De-escalation discussion: the patient was offered enrollment in a post-treatment surveillance de-escalation protocol but declined. Cancer absent (complete response). No evidence of disease.

At age 52 years (approximately 5 months post-treatment), the patient underwent repeat direct laryngoscopy and biopsy of the right base of tongue region of concern identified on MRI (residual 0.9 cm lesion). This was performed under general anesthesia with narrow-band imaging (NBI). Findings: smooth post-radiation mucosa at the right base of tongue with no visible tumor; the 0.9 cm MRI lesion appeared to represent fibrotic tissue. Random biopsies from the base of tongue, right lateral pharyngeal wall, right tonsil fossa (residual cuff), and hypopharynx were all taken. Pathology pending.

At age 52 years (approximately 5 months post-treatment), pathology from direct laryngoscopy biopsies of the right base of tongue and oropharynx returned as follows: Right base of tongue (3 fragments): extensive radiation changes with stromal fibrosis, vascular ectasia, and atypical stromal cells consistent with radiation effect. No viable carcinoma identified. Right lateral pharyngeal wall (2 fragments): squamous mucosa with reactive changes, no malignancy. Right tonsillar fossa (2 fragments): benign lymphoid tissue with post-radiation fibrosis, no malignancy. Hypopharynx (2 fragments): squamous mucosa, no malignancy. Conclusion: No viable squamous cell carcinoma identified. Findings consistent with complete pathologic response to definitive chemoradiation.

At age 52 years (approximately 6 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in oncology follow-up. Pathology from direct laryngoscopy confirmed complete pathologic response - no viable tumor. The patient was counseled that this represents a complete response and that the plan would be ongoing surveillance. Swallowing status: patient now tolerating soft mechanical diet with minimal PEG use (<25% caloric needs). She was hoping to have the PEG tube removed. Xerostomia: grade 1-2 persistent, managed with frequent sips of water, Biotene, salivary substitutes. Voice quality: mild hoarseness, stable. Weight: 68 kg, slowly recovering. ECOG PS: 1. Thyroid function: TSH 6.8 mIU/L (elevated, consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism) - levothyroxine dose increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Plan: continue close surveillance, PEG removal in 4-6 weeks if oral intake adequate. Cancer absent.

At age 52 years (approximately 7 months post-treatment), the patient underwent uncomplicated PEG tube removal by gastroenterology after demonstrating adequate oral caloric intake (>1800 kcal/day orally, weight stable at 69 kg). The PEG site healed without complication. The patient was transitioned to full oral diet (soft mechanical) and continued with speech-language pathology for swallowing rehabilitation targeting advancement to regular diet consistency.

At age 52 years (approximately 9 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent surveillance PET-CT as part of the 9-12 month post-treatment imaging protocol. Results: No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 1.9, consistent with post-treatment changes). No pathologic cervical adenopathy. No distant metastases identified. Impression: No evidence of recurrence or distant metastatic disease. Complete ongoing metabolic response. Stable post-treatment changes in the oropharynx.

At age 53 years (approximately 12 months post-treatment), the patient was evaluated for late radiation toxicities. She reported persistent grade 2 xerostomia (significantly decreased salivary production, dry mouth affecting sleep and diet), grade 1-2 dysphagia (able to manage modified regular diet but avoids dry foods and thick solids), and grade 1 trismus (mouth opening 3.8 cm, baseline estimated 4.5 cm). No radiation-induced carotid stenosis on ultrasound screening. No osteonecrosis of the mandible. Audiogram: high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss (4000-8000 Hz bilaterally), grade 1, likely related to cisplatin. Managed with Biotene, pilocarpine 5 mg TID (initiated for xerostomia with modest benefit), ongoing jaw stretching exercises with TheraBite device, and quarterly speech-language pathology sessions. TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normalized on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily). No features of radiation-induced fibrosis or lymphedema of the neck.

At age 53 years (12 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in annual follow-up in the head and neck survivorship clinic. She reported significantly improved quality of life compared to 6 months post-treatment. She had returned to work as a music teacher at 10 months post-treatment. She was tolerating a regular-modified diet, had regained 3 kg (now 71 kg), and was managing xerostomia with regular pilocarpine and salivary substitutes. Physical examination: no palpable cervical adenopathy, no oral mucosal lesions, oropharynx with post-radiation mucosal changes but no visible tumor. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: post-radiation changes at right base of tongue (smooth, fibrotic mucosa), no visible lesion. ECOG PS: 0. Cancer absent. No evidence of disease at 12 months. Continued surveillance scheduled per NCCN guidelines (clinical exam every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, annually thereafter).

At age 53 years (18 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent MRI of the neck as part of surveillance (18-month imaging checkpoint). Results: Stable post-treatment changes at the right base of tongue. The previously noted 0.9 cm area of fibrosis has decreased to 0.5 cm and demonstrates no enhancement on post-contrast sequences. No new mucosal lesions. Right level IIA node: stable 0.7 cm fibrotic-appearing node, no internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy in the neck. No evidence of local recurrence or new distant metastases. Impression: No radiographic evidence of recurrent disease. Stable/improving post-treatment changes consistent with ongoing fibrosis and resolution.

At age 53 years (18 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in oncology surveillance. MRI stable. No clinical concerns. Physical examination: no palpable adenopathy, flexible nasopharyngoscopy shows stable post-radiation changes, no visible lesion. TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (euthyroid on levothyroxine 100 mcg). She reported continued improvement in swallowing; now tolerating regular diet with only occasional avoidance of dry or sticky foods. Xerostomia: persistent grade 1-2 but manageable. Trismus: improved to 4.1 cm mouth opening with TheraBite exercises. Audiogram: stable, mild high-frequency SNHL bilaterally (grade 1). Pilocarpine continued. No signs of recurrence. Cancer absent at 18 months. Plan: transition to less frequent follow-up (every 4-6 months). Annual thyroid ultrasound initiated (radiation-induced thyroid cancer screening). Discussion of late effects monitoring including carotid artery disease screening given radiation exposure to neck vasculature.

At age 54 years (24 months post-chemoradiation), the patient underwent surveillance PET-CT at the 2-year mark. Results: No evidence of FDG-avid disease at the primary site (right base of tongue) or regional nodes. SUVmax at base of tongue: 1.7 (well within physiologic range). No pathologic adenopathy. No distant metastases. Thyroid: no FDG-avid thyroid nodules. Impression: Complete ongoing metabolic response, no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease at 24 months post-treatment. Excellent prognosis given HPV-positive histology and sustained complete response.

At age 54 years (24 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in oncology follow-up. PET-CT confirmed no evidence of disease at 2 years. This milestone is highly significant for HPV-positive OPSCC, as the majority of late recurrences (>24 months post-treatment) are uncommon in this biology. The patient was counseled that her 5-year locoregional control and overall survival rates are estimated at 85-90% given complete response, HPV-positive status, and never-smoker profile. She was informed that surveillance imaging frequency would be reduced going forward. De-escalation of surveillance to annual imaging and clinical exam every 6 months was discussed and implemented. The patient was also referred to genetics counseling for the germline BRCA2 variant of unknown significance identified on NGS. Quality of life: returned to full professional duties, active lifestyle, minimal limitations. Xerostomia: grade 1, stable. No trismus. No lymphedema. ECOG PS: 0. Cancer absent at 24 months. No evidence of disease.

At age 54 years (30 months post-treatment), the patient was seen in routine survivorship follow-up. No new symptoms. Physical examination unremarkable, no palpable lymphadenopathy, flexible nasopharyngoscopy with no mucosal lesions or visible tumor. Thyroid ultrasound: no thyroid nodules. Carotid artery Doppler ultrasound (first screening, 30 months post-neck irradiation): mild intimal-medial thickening of right common carotid artery at the radiation field, no hemodynamically significant stenosis, <30% stenosis bilaterally. Lipid panel: LDL 118 mg/dL, initiated low-dose statin (atorvastatin 10 mg) per cardiovascular risk reduction guidelines for radiation-exposed carotid arteries. Audiology: stable mild high-frequency SNHL bilaterally (grade 1, unchanged). Pilocarpine dose tapered to 5 mg BID. Genetics: BRCA2 p.D2723H variant reclassified as likely benign by genetics counseling team, no additional BRCA testing warranted for family members at this time. ECOG PS: 0. Cancer absent at 30 months.

documents.text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY / RADIATION ONCOLOGY
JOINT POST-TREATMENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE

ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Radiation Oncology]
VISIT TYPE: Scheduled Post-Treatment Follow-Up (Joint Medical Oncology and Radiation Oncology)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old West African-descent female never-smoker presenting for her first scheduled post-treatment clinic visit following completion of definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 by AJCC 8th edition conventional staging (stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She completed her 35th and final fraction of IMRT approximately 10 days ago and presents today for acute toxicity assessment, nutritional status review, and discussion of the post-treatment surveillance plan.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old music teacher who initially presented with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Workup confirmed an HPV-positive (p16 3+, HPV-16 RNA ISH positive) moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue measuring 3.2 cm with a single right level IIA lymph node at 2.1 cm. Staging PET-CT confirmed no distant metastases. NGS identified a PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation and CDKN2A deletion among other alterations; TMB was low at 6.2 mut/Mb, MSS. After multidisciplinary tumor board review, she was recommended for definitive concurrent chemoradiation rather than upfront surgical resection.

Treatment course: The patient received IMRT using SIB-VMAT technique -- 70 Gy to the high-risk CTV in 35 fractions over 7 weeks, with elective nodal volumes receiving 56-63 Gy per protocol. A PEG tube was placed prophylactically prior to treatment initiation. Concurrent systemic therapy consisted of weekly Platinol at 40 mg/m2; she received 6 of a planned 7 cycles, with cycle 5 held for grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, baseline 0.8 mg/dL) and grade 2 thrombocytopenia. Cumulative Platinol delivered was approximately 414 mg/m2, representing approximately 83% of the planned total dose -- felt to be adequate.

The treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis beginning in week 4, which required a 3-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, aggressive pain management including morphine PCA transitioning to extended-release oxycodone, and initiation of PEG tube feeds. Since that hospitalization, the patient has been wholly dependent on PEG nutrition (Vital 1.5 at 75 mL/hr x 16 hours/day) with minimal to no oral intake due to severe odynophagia. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis over the right neck (managed with Mepitel film and Aquaphor), grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 1 fatigue. No radiation treatment interruptions occurred. Creatinine has since recovered to approximately 1.0 mg/dL on repeat labs obtained at her last visit.

Today, the patient reports that the mucositis is beginning to improve -- she describes the pain as 4-5/10, down from 9/10 at its worst. She is still not able to tolerate oral liquids comfortably but is hopeful about recovery. She is exhausted, still quite fatigued, and endorses low mood related to the prolonged treatment burden and physical deconditioning. She denies new neck masses, fevers, or changes in her voice quality beyond mild hoarseness that has been present throughout treatment. She is currently taking extended-release oxycodone 20 mg BID with oxycodone 5 mg IR q4h PRN for breakthrough pain, with moderate pain control.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Significant fatigue (patient rates 7/10 on functional scale), 9 kg weight loss total over the course of treatment (from 76 kg pre-treatment to 67 kg today), decreased energy and stamina. No fever or chills.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Persistent bilateral xerostomia (markedly reduced saliva since week 3 of radiation), dysgeusia with near-complete loss of taste (everything described as "metallic or like cardboard"), mild bilateral tinnitus (new since Platinol), right-sided odynophagia still present but improving. Mild hoarseness, stable. Trismus not yet formally assessed. No new neck mass. No otalgia.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations. Mild lower extremity edema noted during inpatient stay, resolved.
Respiratory: No dyspnea. No cough. No hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: PEG tube functioning well. No abdominal pain, nausea currently mild, no vomiting. Constipation managed with polyethylene glycol (PEG tube administered).
Musculoskeletal: No neck stiffness. Mild generalized myalgias from deconditioning.
Neurologic: No peripheral numbness or tingling (no neuropathy).
Psychiatric: Low mood, not meeting criteria for major depression per patient report, but distressed about functional losses. Social work engaged.
Endocrine: No new symptoms; on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily for pre-existing hypothyroidism (TSH not yet re-checked post-radiation).
Skin: Right neck radiation field skin: previously moist desquamation, now in re-epithelialization phase, tender but improving.

---

MEDICATIONS

1. Levothyroxine 75 mcg PO daily (pre-existing hypothyroidism)
2. Oxycodone ER (OxyContin) 20 mg PO BID via PEG tube
3. Oxycodone IR 5 mg PO/PEG q4h PRN breakthrough pain
4. Ondansetron 4 mg PO q8h PRN nausea
5. Polyethylene glycol 17 g PO daily via PEG (constipation)
6. Vital 1.5 tube feed formula at 75 mL/hr x 16 hours/day via PEG
7. Magic mouthwash (diphenhydramine 12.5 mg/lidocaine 2%/Maalox) swish-and-spit q4h PRN mucositis
8. Dexamethasone 0.1% oral rinse swish-and-spit TID (mucositis)
9. Aquaphor ointment topically to right neck skin TID
10. Proton pump inhibitor (omeprazole 20 mg daily via PEG)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
- Hypothyroidism (well-controlled pre-treatment on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- HPV-positive right base of tongue SCC, T2N1M0 (stage I HPV-OPC staging) -- current diagnosis
- Grade 2 nephrotoxicity during cis treatment (resolved)

SOCIAL HISTORY: High school music teacher, currently on medical leave. Never-smoker. No alcohol use. West African descent. Monogamous relationship. Lives with partner. Supportive home environment; partner has been primary caregiver during treatment.

FAMILY HISTORY: No family history of head and neck cancers. No family history of BRCA-related cancers known; BRCA2 VUS (p.D2723H) identified on somatic NGS, germline testing pending.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: The patient appears tired and mildly cachectic, though more alert than at her last visit during the inpatient admission. She is in no acute distress. She is speaking in full sentences, though with a mild change in voice quality (slightly muffled/hypernasalized).

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 108/70 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm, temperature 37.0 C, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 67 kg (down 9 kg from baseline of 76 kg).

Head and Neck:
- Oral cavity/oropharynx: Mucositis clearly improving compared to the height of treatment. Residual grade 1-2 patchy pseudomembranous changes visible at the base of tongue and bilateral soft palate; no longer the confluent grade 3 changes seen during admission. The right base of tongue primary site is difficult to fully visualize on mirror exam but the posterior oropharynx appears less edematous. Significant erythema throughout the irradiated oropharyngeal mucosa.
- Neck: Right level II lymph node previously measuring 2.1 cm and palpable at diagnosis -- today no longer palpable. The right anterior neck skin in the radiation field shows patchy re-epithelialization with residual erythema and superficial desquamation in the process of healing; no frank moist desquamation at this visit. No left-sided adenopathy. No palpable submandibular masses. Bilateral neck supple.
- Salivary glands: Parotid region non-tender bilaterally. Submandibular glands non-enlarged.
- Ears: External canals clear bilaterally. Tympanic membranes intact.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Tongue midline on protrusion, no tongue deviation or atrophy. No shoulder droop (CN XI intact). Mild generalized weakness consistent with deconditioning.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No stridor.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs.

Abdomen: PEG site: clean, granulation tissue forming, no infection or leakage. Abdomen soft, non-tender.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No cyanosis.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (from labs drawn today)

- CBC: WBC 5.4 x10^9/L (normal), Hgb 10.8 g/dL (mild anemia, down from 13.2 pre-treatment -- consistent with chemoradiation-associated anemia), platelets 198 x10^9/L (recovered from prior nadirs)
- BMP: Sodium 138, Potassium 3.8, Chloride 102, Bicarbonate 26, BUN 14, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (improved from 1.3 at nadir; eGFR ~62 mL/min, mildly reduced from baseline >90, still trending toward recovery)
- Magnesium 2.0 mg/dL (low-normal; monitoring given prior Platinol-associated hypomagnesemia)
- Phosphorus 3.3 mg/dL (normal)
- Albumin 3.1 g/dL (low; consistent with nutritional deficits and inflammation)
- Prealbumin 14 mg/dL (low; nutritional depletion)
- TSH: 5.2 mIU/L (elevated, above pre-treatment baseline; raising concern for radiation-induced hypothyroidism in addition to pre-existing primary hypothyroidism -- levothyroxine dose adjustment to be considered)
- LFTs: AST, ALT, alkaline phosphatase within normal limits
- Coagulation: INR 1.0

---

IMAGING

No restaging imaging has been performed at this visit -- per standard practice and NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, restaging PET-CT and MRI of the neck are planned at 8-12 weeks post-completion of chemoradiation to allow sufficient time for inflammatory changes to resolve and to maximize sensitivity/specificity for detecting residual disease. Imaging will be ordered and scheduled for approximately 8-10 weeks from today.

Noted on clinical examination: The previously palpable right level II lymph node is no longer palpable. This is an encouraging early clinical indicator but is not sufficient to confirm response in lieu of formal restaging imaging.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old never-smoker with HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0 (stage I HPV-OPC staging), who has just completed her definitive concurrent chemoradiation course -- 70 Gy IMRT with 6 of 7 planned weekly Platinol cycles (cumulative ~414 mg/m2, approximately 83% planned dose). She is seen here approximately 10 days after her final radiation fraction.

1. TREATMENT COMPLETION AND EARLY RESPONSE ASSESSMENT:
The patient has completed definitive treatment. Clinically, the previously palpable right level IIA node is no longer palpable, which is an encouraging early sign. Formal restaging with PET-CT and MRI of the neck will be scheduled for 8-10 weeks post-treatment completion per standard practice. We will review those results together. The patient was counseled that it is too early at this visit to make a definitive statement about tumor response, and that she should not interpret the improving symptoms alone as confirmed remission. She understands.

2. MUCOSITIS (GRADE 1-2, IMPROVING FROM GRADE 3 NADIR):
Mucositis is resolving as expected in the weeks following radiation completion. The peak was grade 3, requiring hospitalization in week 4 -- this has now improved to grade 1-2. Continue magic mouthwash and dexamethasone oral rinse for comfort. Continue oxycodone ER for background pain management; will plan opioid dose taper beginning in 2-3 weeks as mucositis continues to resolve -- will start by decreasing IR breakthrough frequency, then taper ER dose. Counseled on bowel regimen given opioid use. Continue PPI.

3. NUTRITIONAL STATUS AND PEG TUBE:
The patient has lost 9 kg (12% of pre-treatment body weight). She remains fully PEG-dependent at this time. Albumin and prealbumin are low, consistent with significant nutritional depletion. Will continue current PEG tube feeds (Vital 1.5 at goal rate) and involve dietetics for formal reassessment. Target caloric intake remains ~2200 kcal/day. The patient was counseled that PEG removal is not appropriate at this time and that oral intake should be reintroduced gradually as mucositis resolves and swallowing function recovers. She expressed strong motivation to regain the ability to eat normally -- this is an important quality-of-life priority.

4. SWALLOWING REHABILITATION:
Speech-language pathology to begin formal swallowing rehabilitation this week. This will include: thermal-tactile stimulation exercises, effortful swallowing exercises, Mendelsohn maneuver training, and supraglottic swallow techniques. The goal is to prevent post-radiation dysphagia from worsening due to fibrosis and to begin the process of oral diet reintroduction. A formal FEES (functional endoscopic evaluation of swallowing) will be scheduled at 4-6 weeks post-treatment to assess swallowing mechanics and guide diet advancement.

5. RADIATION DERMATITIS (HEALING, RIGHT NECK):
The right neck skin is in the re-epithelialization phase, improving from grade 2 moist desquamation. Continue Aquaphor topically TID. Avoid sun exposure and occlusive dressings. No antibiotics required at this time. Skin should fully heal within 3-4 weeks.

6. NEPHROTOXICITY (RECOVERING):
Creatinine now 1.0 mg/dL, down from nadir of 1.3 mg/dL. eGFR approximately 62 mL/min, still below pre-treatment baseline of >90 mL/min. No further cis is planned. Will monitor creatinine at next visit. Patient to maintain adequate oral hydration as she transitions off PEG. Given mildly depressed eGFR, avoid NSAIDs (patient counseled). Low threshold to recheck renal function if patient becomes symptomatic.

7. HYPOMAGNESEMIA (BORDERLINE) AND ANEMIA:
Magnesium 2.0 mg/dL -- borderline low. Will supplement with magnesium oxide 400 mg PO BID via PEG until dietary intake recovers. Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb 10.8) consistent with chemoradiation effect; no indication for erythropoietin or transfusion at this level. Expect gradual recovery over 3-6 months. Recheck CBC at next visit.

8. HYPOTHYROIDISM (TSH ELEVATED):
TSH now 5.2 mIU/L, elevated above her pre-treatment well-controlled baseline on levothyroxine 75 mcg. This is consistent with radiation-induced direct thyroid gland injury (the thyroid gland sits partially within the lower neck irradiation fields) superimposed on pre-existing primary hypothyroidism. Increasing levothyroxine from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Will recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks. Patient counseled about signs/symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation) and to contact us if she notices worsening. She will need ongoing thyroid function monitoring and may require further levothyroxine adjustments; thyroid ultrasound will be incorporated into long-term surveillance.

9. TINNITUS / OTOTOXICITY MONITORING:
Mild bilateral tinnitus noted by patient since Platinol treatment. Monitoring audiogram was performed after the 4th Platinol cycle and was stable per prior records. A formal audiogram will be repeated at approximately 3 months post-treatment. Patient counseled on Platinol-associated sensorineural hearing loss and the importance of hearing protection going forward.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL:
Patient appears emotionally strained by the cumulative toll of treatment. Low mood, significant functional losses (dietary, vocal), and prolonged medical leave from her work as a music teacher (which she clearly values greatly). Social work is actively following. She was reassured that her clinical trajectory appears favorable -- completing treatment without interruption and with adequate cis delivery is a good prognostic indicator in HPV-positive OPSCC. We discussed the expected timeline for recovery of swallowing, taste, and energy over the coming 3-6 months. She was encouraged and expressed optimism about returning to work.

11. GERMLINE GENETICS:
Patient was reminded about the BRCA2 VUS (p.D2723H, AF 48% on somatic NGS, likely germline) identified at the time of tumor NGS. Referral to cancer genetics was placed at the time of tumor board, and she was encouraged to follow up with genetics counseling once she has regained strength after treatment. This does not change current management but is important for long-term risk stratification.

12. SURVEILLANCE PLANNING:
Formal restaging imaging will be ordered now:
- PET-CT (18F-FDG) at approximately 10 weeks post-treatment completion
- MRI neck with and without contrast at approximately 10 weeks post-treatment (concurrent with PET-CT)
Follow-up visit after imaging review will be scheduled with both medical oncology and radiation oncology. Per NCCN HPV-positive OPSCC surveillance guidelines, clinic visits are recommended every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, and annually thereafter beyond year 5. Annual thyroid function testing is mandatory given prior neck irradiation.

Return to clinic: 2 weeks for toxicity check-in and labs, then after restaging imaging is completed at approximately 10 weeks post-treatment.

ECOG Performance Status: 2 (limited self-care ability, in bed or chair less than 50% of waking hours -- primarily due to fatigue, pain, and inability to eat orally)

Cancer Status: Indeterminate -- too early post-treatment to assess response. Restaging imaging pending.

Time spent with patient: 55 minutes. Greater than 50% of this time was spent counseling and coordinating care.

18F-FDG PET/CT WHOLE BODY WITH DIAGNOSTIC NECK CT

EXAMINATION: Whole-body PET/CT with 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG), with dedicated diagnostic-quality contrast-enhanced CT of the neck.

INDICATION: 52-year-old female with recently completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation (cis-based chemotherapy and IMRT, 70 Gy to primary site, 7 weeks of treatment, completed approximately 10 weeks prior to this examination) for HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition). Restaging examination to evaluate treatment response and assess for residual or recurrent locoregional disease and distant metastatic spread.

COMPARISON: Pre-treatment staging PET-CT (obtained prior to initiation of chemoradiation). Pre-treatment staging MRI neck (same date as prior PET-CT).

TECHNIQUE: Following a fasting period of at least 6 hours with measured blood glucose of 88 mg/dL, the patient received 12.3 mCi of 18F-FDG intravenously via peripheral IV access in the left antecubital fossa. Uptake period of 60 minutes in a quiet, warm, dimly lit room with patient at rest. Whole-body PET/CT acquisition performed from vertex to mid-thigh in the supine position. Dedicated neck CT performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (Isovue-370, 100 mL, weight-based dosing, no adverse reaction). PET data reconstructed using iterative algorithm (3D OSEM) with time-of-flight correction and post-filter smoothing. Attenuation correction was performed using the CT data. Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes on a dedicated PET/CT fusion workstation. Maximum standardized uptake values (SUVmax) reported utilizing lean body mass correction.

RADIATION DOSE: Total effective dose approximately 14.2 mSv (combined PET and CT components).

--- FINDINGS ---

HEAD AND NECK — PRIMARY SITE:
The right base of tongue, previously the site of an intensely FDG-avid exophytic mass with SUVmax of 14.2 on the pre-treatment staging PET-CT, now demonstrates markedly diminished FDG activity. There is faint, diffuse, symmetric mucosal uptake across the oropharynx with the most prominent uptake at the right base of tongue measuring SUVmax 2.1, which falls well within the expected range for post-radiation inflammatory mucosal change and physiologic activity of residual lymphoid tissue. No discrete, focal, or nodular region of intense hypermetabolic activity is identified at the primary site. On the fused PET/CT and diagnostic neck CT, the previously defined bulky enhancing mass is no longer identifiable as a discrete soft-tissue structure. The base of tongue mucosa is thickened diffusely, consistent with radiation-related mucosal edema and early fibrotic change, without evidence of a residual mass. The oral tongue, floor of mouth, soft palate, uvula, hard palate, and buccal mucosae are unremarkable without discrete FDG-avid lesions.

The right tonsillar fossa and right lateral pharyngeal wall demonstrate mild diffuse mucosal thickening with low-level, non-specific FDG uptake (SUVmax approximately 2.3), consistent with post-radiation inflammatory change and without morphologic or metabolic features to suggest viable tumor.

The nasopharynx, posterior pharyngeal wall, piriform sinuses, and hypopharynx appear symmetric without focal FDG-avid mucosal lesion. The larynx including the supraglottis, glottis, and subglottis shows no evidence of FDG-avid disease.

RETROPHARYNGEAL REGION:
No pathologically enlarged or hypermetabolic retropharyngeal lymph nodes. Bilateral retropharyngeal regions are within normal limits.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The parotid glands are slightly reduced in volume bilaterally, slightly more pronounced on the right, consistent with post-radiation atrophic change. There is mild diffuse FDG uptake bilaterally at physiologic levels. No discrete FDG-avid parotid lesion. The submandibular glands are mildly atrophic bilaterally, symmetrically, consistent with radiation effect; no FDG-avid lesion. The sublingual glands and minor salivary glands are without discrete abnormality.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES — RIGHT NECK:
The right level IIA cervical lymph node that was previously hypermetabolic (SUVmax 9.8, measuring 2.1 cm with central necrosis on prior staging PET-CT) is now substantially diminished in size and metabolic activity. The node is identified on the current CT as a small, ovoid, mildly hyperdense structure measuring approximately 0.8 x 0.6 cm in axial dimension, without internal necrosis, without irregular rim enhancement, and without the peripheral ring-enhancement pattern that characterized it on the prior CT. FDG activity corresponding to this node measures SUVmax 1.8, which is at background/physiologic levels and does not exceed mediastinal blood pool activity. This finding is consistent with marked treatment response, with the residual nodal tissue most likely representing post-treatment fibrosis, sclerosis, or a small reactive remnant lymph node.

No additional right-sided cervical adenopathy is identified at levels I, IIB, III, IV, or V. Right level VI and VII stations are negative.

CERVICAL LYMPH NODES — LEFT NECK:
No pathologic adenopathy identified in the left neck at levels I through VI. No new contralateral cervical nodal disease has developed since the prior examination.

SUPRACLAVICULAR AND INFRACLAVICULAR REGIONS:
No hypermetabolic or enlarged lymph nodes in the bilateral supraclavicular or infraclavicular regions.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland is mildly heterogeneous in attenuation, likely reflecting early radiation-related thyroid parenchymal change given the patient's prior neck irradiation (70 Gy to primary site, with the thyroid gland partially within the radiation treatment volume). No discrete FDG-avid thyroid nodule. A small 6-mm hypoattenuating focus in the left thyroid lobe is identified on CT (too small to characterize); this is non-FDG avid and is likely a benign colloid cyst or small follicular nodule. Clinical correlation with thyroid function studies and thyroid ultrasound is noted (the clinical team should be aware of potential radiation-induced thyroid dysfunction given radiation exposure).

CHEST:
LUNGS: The lungs are well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodule, mass, or consolidation identified above the threshold of detection. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. The central airways are patent. Mediastinum is midline. No FDG-avid pulmonary lesion.

MEDIASTINAL AND HILAR LYMPH NODES: No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. No FDG-avid mediastinal or hilar adenopathy.

HEART AND PERICARDIUM: The cardiac silhouette is within normal limits. No pericardial effusion. The great vessels appear patent.

BONY THORAX: No FDG-avid osseous lesion in the ribs, sternum, thoracic spine, or clavicles.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
LIVER: The liver parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation with no focal hepatic lesion identified. There is a tiny 5 mm hypodense focus in hepatic segment IV, too small to characterize on CT, without corresponding FDG uptake above background; this likely represents a benign cyst or incidental finding. No biliary ductal dilatation.

SPLEEN: Unremarkable. No splenic lesion. Spleen size within normal limits.

PANCREAS: The visualized pancreas is unremarkable. No pancreatic mass or ductal abnormality.

ADRENAL GLANDS: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in morphology and without FDG-avid lesion. No adrenal mass.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS: The kidneys are symmetric in size and morphology with normal cortical thickness. No suspicious renal lesion. Mild, symmetric cortical thinning within normal limits for age. There is bilateral physiologic FDG excretion into the collecting systems. No hydroureteronephrosis.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT: Physiologic FDG activity in the bowel is noted. No focal colonic or gastric FDG-avid lesion. The stomach, small bowel, and large bowel appear unremarkable. A PEG tube is identified in the appropriate gastric position (known, placed prior to chemoradiation), with no surrounding inflammatory change or abnormal FDG activity about the tube site.

PERITONEUM AND RETROPERITONEUM: No peritoneal nodularity or FDG-avid implants. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

BLADDER AND PELVIC ORGANS: Dense physiologic FDG activity in the bladder from urinary excretion of tracer. Uterus and adnexa are within normal limits for age. No pelvic adenopathy. No adnexal mass or FDG-avid pelvic lesion.

SKELETON:
No focal FDG-avid osseous lesion to suggest lytic or sclerotic metastatic disease. No destructive bone lesion identified in the axial or appendicular skeleton within the field of view. No new periosteal reaction or cortical disruption.

SOFT TISSUES:
No abnormal soft tissue mass or FDG-avid subcutaneous lesion. Diffuse mild subcutaneous edema and skin thickening of the right lateral neck and right posterior neck is identified, corresponding to the prior radiation field, consistent with radiation-induced skin and subcutaneous change (post-radiation dermatitis/fibrosis). No abnormal FDG-avid soft tissue lesion.

--- IMPRESSION ---

1. Complete metabolic response at the primary site: The previously intensely hypermetabolic right base of tongue primary tumor (pre-treatment SUVmax 14.2) has resolved with no discrete FDG-avid lesion now identified. Residual low-level symmetric oropharyngeal mucosal FDG activity (SUVmax 2.1) is within the expected range for post-radiation mucosal inflammation and physiologic activity, without morphologic or metabolic features suspicious for residual viable tumor.

2. Complete metabolic response at the previously involved right level IIA lymph node: The formerly necrotic, hypermetabolic right level IIA lymph node (prior SUVmax 9.8, 2.1 cm) has dramatically diminished in both size (now 0.8 cm) and metabolic activity (SUVmax 1.8, at background/physiologic levels), consistent with a robust nodal treatment response. Imaging appearances are most in keeping with post-treatment fibrosis/sclerosis of the prior metastatic node.

3. No new locoregional disease: No evidence of nodal recurrence or new cervical adenopathy in the bilateral neck, bilateral supraclavicular regions, or retropharyngeal stations.

4. No distant metastatic disease: No FDG-avid lesion identified in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis to suggest distant hematogenous spread.

5. Post-treatment inflammatory changes in the oropharynx and right neck soft tissues are noted, consistent with expected changes following high-dose definitive radiation therapy.

6. Incidental findings: Mildly heterogeneous thyroid parenchyma with possible early radiation-induced change; small non-avid left thyroid focus (likely benign); tiny non-avid hepatic hypodensity (likely benign cyst). PEG tube in appropriate position.

Overall, the imaging findings at this 10-week post-treatment interval are highly favorable, demonstrating complete resolution of metabolic activity at both the primary tumor site and the previously involved regional lymph node, without evidence of residual or recurrent locoregional disease or distant metastatic spread. These findings are consistent with an excellent early response to definitive concurrent chemoradiation in the setting of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — POST-TREATMENT RESTAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female with a known history of p16-positive (HPV-associated) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging; stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She completed definitive concurrent chemoradiation — intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) via volumetric modulated arc therapy to 70 Gy in 35 fractions at the primary site and positive right level IIA node, with concurrent weekly Platinol 40 mg/m2 — approximately ten weeks prior to this examination. This study is obtained for anatomic post-treatment restaging and correlation with a concurrent restaging PET-CT, which demonstrated complete metabolic response with no residual FDG-avid disease.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the neck was performed on a 3.0 Tesla scanner utilizing a dedicated head-and-neck surface coil array. Sequences obtained include: axial and coronal T1-weighted spin echo (pre-contrast), axial fat-suppressed T2-weighted fast spin echo (STIR), axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping (b-values 0, 500, 1000 s/mm2), and axial, sagittal, and coronal T1-weighted fat-saturated sequences acquired following intravenous administration of gadobutrol (Gadavist) 0.1 mmol/kg, injected via right antecubital access at 2 mL/s. No contraindications to gadolinium. No prior allergic reactions reported. Comparison is made to the pre-treatment baseline MRI of the neck performed at the time of initial staging.


FINDINGS:

Primary Site — Right Base of Tongue:
On the pre-treatment baseline study, a 3.2 x 2.8 cm exophytic, avidly enhancing soft tissue mass was centered at the right base of tongue, extending toward the right tonsillar fossa but with preserved vallecula and pre-epiglottic fat.

On the current examination, there has been marked interval reduction in the right base of tongue soft tissue abnormality. In the region of the known primary tumor, there is now a small residual area of mucosal thickening and submucosal signal abnormality measuring approximately 0.9 x 0.7 cm in the axial plane (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm), representing a dramatic reduction in bulk from the pre-treatment baseline. This residual focus demonstrates heterogeneously reduced T2 signal intensity compared to the original mass, with low-signal fibrous stranding on T2-weighted sequences. On post-contrast imaging, there is faint, mildly heterogeneous enhancement within this residual focus, substantially diminished in degree and extent compared to the pre-treatment avid enhancement pattern. On DWI, there is no convincing restricted diffusion; ADC values within this residual area are elevated (approximately 1.3 x 10^-3 mm2/s), favoring treatment-related fibrosis over viable tumor, though a small component of residual microscopic disease cannot be entirely excluded by imaging alone.

The right tonsillar fossa: no discrete enhancing soft tissue mass. Post-radiation mucosal irregularity noted, without a definable nodule or mass. The glossotonsillar sulcus on the right appears intact with fibrous remodeling. No extension to the vallecula. The pre-epiglottic fat plane is preserved and shows no infiltrating soft tissue.

There is diffuse post-radiation signal change throughout the oropharyngeal soft tissues bilaterally: increased T2 signal and slight mucosal thickening involving the pharyngeal walls and soft palate mucosa, consistent with post-radiation inflammatory and edematous changes. The parapharyngeal fat shows mild bilateral symmetric edematous change, greater on the right — expected in the setting of recent IMRT.

Mandible and Skull Base:
No cortical erosion, marrow infiltration, or periosteal reaction within the mandible, floor of mouth, or skull base. The mandibular cortex is intact. The pterygopalatine fossa and infratemporal fossa are clear of tumor extension. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
Right Level IIA (previously positive, 2.1 x 1.8 cm, with internal necrosis and peripheral rim enhancement): The previously abnormal right level IIA lymph node has markedly decreased in size and now measures 0.8 x 0.6 cm. The internal necrotic area previously identified is no longer present. The node demonstrates homogeneous, low-to-intermediate T2 signal without internal fluid signal, and shows no pathologic enhancement on post-contrast sequences — the enhancement pattern is symmetric with and indistinguishable from adjacent small reactive nodes. The ADC value within this node is elevated (approximately 1.5 x 10^-3 mm2/s), consistent with fibrotic replacement rather than viable metastatic tissue. Overall, the morphologic appearance is consistent with a treated, fibrotic lymph node without imaging evidence of residual viable metastatic adenopathy.

No new pathologic lymphadenopathy is identified in the right neck at levels I through V. No evidence of extracapsular soft tissue extension at any nodal station. Contralateral (left) neck: several small lymph nodes are visible at level II and III bilaterally, none meeting size criteria for pathologic enlargement, with normal fatty hila and no abnormal enhancement. No retropharyngeal adenopathy. No posterior cervical space adenopathy. No supraclavicular or low cervical level VI adenopathy.

Salivary Glands:
The right parotid gland demonstrates slightly reduced volume compared to the pre-treatment baseline, with mildly heterogeneous T1 signal and reduced fat replacement, consistent with early radiation-induced parenchymal changes. The left parotid gland is relatively preserved in volume. Both submandibular glands are mildly edematous in signal but without focal lesion. No obstructing calculi or ductal dilatation identified.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is within the lower field of view. It appears mildly heterogeneous in echotexture on T2-weighted sequences but without discrete focal nodule or mass. No suspicious enhancement. Clinical correlation with thyroid function tests and ultrasound is noted to be ongoing given prior irradiation of the lower neck.

Vascular Structures:
The bilateral carotid arteries and internal jugular veins are patent and demonstrate normal flow voids. No vascular encasement or displacement by soft tissue. No periarterial soft tissue thickening.

Airway:
The oropharyngeal airway is patent. No soft tissue encroachment from the base of tongue region. The epiglottis and aryepiglottic folds appear unremarkable; no supraglottic soft tissue mass.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
Diffuse subcutaneous edema and mild skin thickening are noted throughout the right neck soft tissues within the prior radiation field, particularly along the right lateral and posterior neck — consistent with post-radiation soft tissue change and skin thickening. No organized fluid collection, abscess, or drainable hematoma. No evidence of osteoradionecrosis or soft tissue necrosis.

Cranial Base and Imaged Intracranial Structures:
The imaged portions of the posterior fossa and skull base are unremarkable. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No calvarial lytic lesion within the field of view.

Perineural Spread:
No imaging evidence of perineural tumor spread along the lingual nerve, inferior alveolar nerve, or hypoglossal nerve. Neural foramina appear patent and of normal caliber.


IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval reduction of the right base of tongue primary tumor, now represented by a small residual submucosal focus measuring 0.9 x 0.7 cm (previously 3.2 x 2.8 cm). The markedly diminished enhancement and elevated ADC values within this residual focus are most consistent with post-treatment fibrosis and scar tissue; however, complete exclusion of microscopic residual tumor by MRI alone is limited. Clinical and endoscopic correlation is recommended.

2. Dramatic response at the previously FDG-avid right level IIA metastatic lymph node, now substantially reduced to 0.8 cm, with complete resolution of internal necrosis and normalization of enhancement characteristics. Appearance is most consistent with fibrotic replacement; no imaging features of residual viable nodal metastasis.

3. No new cervical, retropharyngeal, or contralateral cervical adenopathy identified.

4. No distant disease within the field of view. No evidence of bone marrow involvement of the mandible or skull base.

5. Diffuse post-radiation inflammatory soft tissue and mucosal changes throughout the right oropharynx and neck, appearing expected for approximately ten weeks following completion of high-dose IMRT.

6. Early radiation-induced changes in the right parotid gland; ongoing salivary function monitoring warranted.

7. These findings are concordant with the concurrent restaging PET-CT demonstrating complete metabolic response. Taken together, the overall picture is highly favorable and consistent with a near-complete to complete radiographic response to definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female, never-smoker, of West African descent, who presents for multidisciplinary oncology follow-up approximately 3 months after completing definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (Stage III per AJCC 8th edition conventional staging; Stage I per HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She has had restaging PET-CT and MRI of the neck obtained within the past 2 weeks and presents today for results review and ongoing management planning.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu originally presented roughly 4 months ago with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Workup revealed an exophytic, friable 3.2 cm mass at the right base of tongue with a single right level IIA lymph node measuring 2.1 cm on MRI. Biopsy via direct laryngoscopy confirmed invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with strong, diffuse block-like p16 positivity in greater than 75% of tumor cells; HPV RNA in situ hybridization was positive for high-risk HPV type 16. Staging PET-CT confirmed FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 14.2) and the right level IIA node (SUVmax 9.8) with no distant metastases.

After extensive multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the patient was recommended for definitive concurrent chemoradiation rather than upfront surgery. She underwent prophylactic PEG tube placement in anticipation of nutritional compromise, then began IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk clinical target volume, with appropriately dosed intermediate- and low-risk fields) with concurrent weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2. She completed all 35 fractions of radiation without any treatment interruption, though she required one cisplatin hold in week 5 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.3 mg/dL, which recovered to 1.0 mg/dL with aggressive IV hydration) and grade 2 thrombocytopenia. She ultimately received 6 of 7 planned cisplatin cycles, for a cumulative dose of approximately 414 mg/m2 — approximately 83% of the intended dose, which is generally considered oncologically adequate.

The course of treatment was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis in week 4, requiring a 3-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, electrolyte repletion (hypomagnesemia corrected with IV magnesium sulfate), and initiation of opioid analgesia (PCA with morphine, transitioned to extended-release oxycodone with immediate-release breakthrough dosing). PEG tube feeds were started during that hospitalization at goal rate of Vital 1.5 at 75 mL/hr for 16 hours per day. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the right neck, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 1 fatigue during treatment. She completed radiation without further admissions.

She was seen immediately post-treatment with a total weight loss of 9 kg (12% of baseline) and was fully PEG-dependent at that time. The right level II neck node was no longer palpable on clinical exam after treatment. ECOG performance status at that visit was 2 due to fatigue, pain, and dysphagia. Swallowing rehabilitation was initiated with speech-language pathology.

Restaging imaging was obtained at approximately 10 weeks post-treatment:
- PET-CT showed a complete metabolic response, with no FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 2.1, physiologic range) or the right level IIA nodal station (previously FDG-avid node now 0.8 cm fibrotic-appearing, SUVmax 1.8). No new areas of FDG avid disease and no distant metastases.
- MRI neck with and without contrast showed significant interval reduction of the right base of tongue lesion from 3.2 cm to 0.9 x 0.7 cm with decreased enhancement and T2 signal, most consistent with post-treatment fibrosis or scar tissue; the right level IIA node is now 0.8 cm without internal necrosis or pathologic enhancement.

She presents today in clinic for integration of these results and multidisciplinary management planning.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-positive (p16+) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0 — COMPLETE METABOLIC RESPONSE to definitive concurrent chemoradiation.

I reviewed today's imaging in detail with Ms. Osei-Bonsu, along with her husband who accompanied her to this visit. The PET-CT, which is our most functionally informative study, shows a complete metabolic response with no residual FDG-avid disease at either the primary site or the regional lymph node. The MRI corroborates this finding — the residual 0.9 cm lesion at the base of tongue demonstrates features most consistent with fibrosis/scar and shows no pathologic enhancement. This is an outstanding result.

I want to be transparent with the patient about the small residual anatomic lesion on MRI: the vast majority of such lesions in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC patients represent treatment-related fibrosis rather than viable tumor, particularly when concordantly PET-negative. In the context of our multidisciplinary discussion, the tumor board's consensus is that this lesion is almost certainly post-treatment scar tissue. However, given the size and the small but non-zero possibility of residual viable tumor, the tumor board has recommended direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia to definitively characterize this finding. This will be performed at approximately 5 months post-treatment (approximately 6-8 weeks from now), allowing further maturation of any residual inflammatory changes while not delaying pathologic confirmation unreasonably.

- No adjuvant systemic therapy is indicated at this time. Her imaging response and HPV-positive biology are highly favorable markers. The published literature (RTOG 0129, ECOG-ACRIN 3311) strongly supports the favorable prognosis of complete responders in this patient population.
- She was offered enrollment in a post-treatment surveillance de-escalation research protocol but declined at this time; this will be revisited.
- Surveillance plan per NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC: clinical examination every 1-3 months in year 1, every 2-6 months in year 2, then annually. Annual imaging (MRI or PET-CT) planned at the 1-year and 2-year marks at minimum, with flexible individualization based on clinical course.

2. Residual 0.9 cm right base of tongue lesion on MRI — recommendation for direct laryngoscopy and biopsy under general anesthesia at approximately 5 months post-treatment (4-6 weeks from today). Referral placed to head and neck surgery.

3. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism — TSH elevated at 6.8 mIU/L (up from well-controlled baseline on levothyroxine 75 mcg prior to treatment). This is a recognized late effect of neck irradiation and was anticipated. Levothyroxine dose increased from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily. Repeat TSH in 6 weeks. Patient counseled that ongoing thyroid monitoring will be required and that late hypothyroidism is common after head and neck radiation.

4. Nutritional status and PEG tube management — Patient is currently using the PEG tube for approximately 50% of her caloric needs, supplementing nighttime enteral feeds with oral intake of pureed and soft foods during the day. Weight is stable at 67 kg (9 kg below pre-treatment weight). Albumin mildly reduced at 3.4 g/dL. Plan to maintain PEG tube for now and reassess removal readiness at the next visit. Dietetics goal: advance oral caloric intake to >75% of needs over the next 4-6 weeks. Continuation of Vital 1.5 via PEG at reduced rate overnight as a safety net. Target for PEG removal: patient consistently meeting >1800 kcal/day orally with stable or improving weight.

5. Swallowing rehabilitation — Patient is working actively with speech-language pathology and has advanced to a pureed diet with soft-solid tolerance. FEES is scheduled for repeat assessment in 4 weeks by SLP. She continues daily tongue base retraction and pharyngeal strengthening exercises (Effortful Swallow, Mendelsohn maneuver), thermal-tactile stimulation, and head-lift exercises (Shaker protocol variant). Goal: advancement to soft mechanical diet within 4-6 weeks, followed by progressive trial of regular diet consistency.

6. Xerostomia and oral hygiene — Grade 1-2 persistent xerostomia. Managing with frequent sips of water, Biotene mouthwash and gel, salivary substitute spray (Numoisyn Liquid), and fluoride trays (ongoing dental follow-up every 3 months given radiation exposure). Consideration of initiating pilocarpine (Salagen) 5 mg TID if xerostomia remains bothersome at next visit — discussed with patient today. Patient deferred initiation for now, preferring to trial conservative measures first.

7. Mild anemia — Hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL, improving from nadir. Iron studies to be drawn today (ferritin, serum iron, TIBC) to assess for iron deficiency as contributing etiology in setting of reduced oral intake. B12 and folate to be checked as well. No transfusion indicated at current level.

8. Hypomagnesemia — Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL, low-normal, likely persisting from cisplatin-related renal magnesium wasting. Continue oral magnesium supplementation (magnesium oxide 400 mg BID). Recheck magnesium with next labs in 6 weeks.

9. Hearing — No new complaints of tinnitus or significant hearing loss reported. A post-treatment audiogram is scheduled in the next 4-6 weeks given cumulative cisplatin exposure (414 mg/m2) and the known risk of delayed cisplatin-associated ototoxicity.

10. BRCA2 germline variant — NGS testing identified a BRCA2 p.D2723H variant (allele frequency 48%, raising concern for germline origin). Patient was counseled about the importance of formal germline genetic counseling and has been referred to the Cancer Genetics clinic. Appointment pending. No change in management until germline status is formally characterized.

She was reassured extensively today. Resources provided including survivorship care plan documentation, referral to the head and neck cancer survivor support group, and connection with the oncology social worker for additional support as she transitions back to work. COMMENT:
------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A (right base of tongue) demonstrates the characteristic histopathologic features of radiation-induced tissue injury. Squamous epithelium overlying the biopsy site shows moderate epithelial reactive atypia with nuclear enlargement, occasional multinucleation, and prominent nucleoli; however, the chromatin pattern is smudgy and degenerative rather than hyperchromatic and mitotically active, and there is no architectural distortion or stromal invasion to suggest viable carcinoma. The lamina propria is markedly expanded by dense collagenous fibrosis with hyalinization consistent with post-radiation stromal remodeling. Dilated, ectatic blood vessels with thickened, fibrinoid walls are prominent throughout the dermis and submucosa, consistent with radiation-induced vascular injury. Scattered large, bizarre stromal cells with irregular hyperchromatic nuclei and abundant cytoplasm are noted; these are consistent with radiation-induced fibroblastic atypia (radiation fibroblasts) and are NOT interpreted as residual or recurrent carcinoma given the absence of keratinization, intercellular bridges, invasive growth, or desmoplastic stromal response. Scattered chronic inflammatory infiltrate (predominantly lymphocytes and plasma cells) is present. No ghost cells, keratin pearls, or necrotic tumor nests to suggest prior viable squamous carcinoma are identified on routine H&E sections. Mucicarmine stain: negative (no mucin production). p16 immunohistochemistry performed on specimen A: focal weak cytoplasmic staining only in scattered inflammatory and reactive stromal cells; no diffuse block-like nuclear/cytoplasmic staining in any epithelial population, which would be required to interpret as HPV-related. This pattern is not consistent with residual HPV-associated carcinoma (see Ancillary Studies).

Specimens B, C, and D demonstrate reactive mucosal changes within the radiation field, including mild epithelial reactive atypia, submucosal edema, and vascular ectasia. Specimen C (right tonsillar fossa) contains small residual lymphoid aggregates with preserved germinal center architecture admixed with extensive fibrous replacement; no atypical lymphoid population or carcinoma is identified. No perineural invasion, lymphovascular invasion, or pathologic lymph node tissue is identified in any specimen.

Correlation with concurrent intraoperative findings (narrow-band imaging: smooth post-radiation mucosa, no mucosal irregularity or NBI-positive vascular atypia at right base of tongue) and post-treatment imaging (PET-CT: complete metabolic response; MRI: 0.9 cm residual fibrotic lesion without pathologic enhancement) is recommended and is consistent with the interpretation of complete pathologic response.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old West African female, never-smoker, music teacher, who presents for oncology follow-up approximately 6 months after completing definitive concurrent intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) and weekly cisplatin for p16-positive (HPV-associated) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging, stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She returns today for review of her most recent biopsy pathology results and ongoing management of post-treatment sequelae. ---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Osei-Bonsu initially presented approximately 10 months ago with a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. Workup revealed an exophytic 3.2-cm mass at the right base of tongue with right level IIA nodal metastasis (2.1 cm with internal necrosis on MRI), confirmed on biopsy as moderately differentiated invasive squamous cell carcinoma, p16 strongly positive (>75% of cells, block-like staining), HPV type 16 by RNA in situ hybridization. PET-CT at staging showed SUVmax 14.2 at the primary, 9.8 at the right IIA node, with no distant metastatic disease. NGS revealed a PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation, TERT promoter mutation, FAT1 nonsense mutation, CCND1 amplification, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; MSS, TMB low at 6.2 mut/Mb, PD-L1 CPS 12.

The patient was discussed at the head and neck multidisciplinary tumor board and recommended for definitive concurrent chemoradiation. She underwent prophylactic PEG tube placement prior to initiating treatment. She completed IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique: 70 Gy/35 fractions to the high-risk CTV (primary + right IIA node), 63 Gy/35 fractions to the intermediate-risk CTV, and 56 Gy/35 fractions to the low-risk elective nodal regions. Concurrent chemotherapy was given as weekly cisplatin 40 mg/m2; she received 6 of 7 planned cycles (week 5 held for grade 2 nephrotoxicity and thrombocytopenia), cumulative delivered dose approximately 414 mg/m2.

Her treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis during week 4, requiring a 3-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, pain management (PCA morphine transitioning to extended-release oxycodone), and initiation of PEG tube feeds. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the right neck, grade 2 xerostomia, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 1 fatigue, and grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine peak 1.3 mg/dL, now recovering). Treatment was completed without further interruptions. She lost a total of 9 kg (12% of pre-treatment body weight) during the treatment course.

At the 3-month post-treatment visit, restaging PET-CT demonstrated complete metabolic response: no FDG-avid disease at the primary site (SUVmax 2.1, physiologic) or the previously involved right level IIA lymph node (previously SUVmax 9.8, now 1.8 with no FDG uptake). MRI neck at that same time showed the right base of tongue mass markedly reduced to 0.9 x 0.7 cm with decreased enhancement, felt to represent post-treatment fibrosis or minimal residual change. Given the PET complete response, MRI near-complete response, and the radiographic uncertainty of the residual 0.9-cm MRI signal, the multidisciplinary team recommended direct laryngoscopy with biopsy approximately 5 months post-treatment for tissue confirmation. This was performed under general anesthesia with narrow-band imaging (NBI) approximately 3-4 weeks ago. Gross inspection showed smooth, post-radiation mucosa at the base of tongue with no visible tumor; random biopsies from the right base of tongue, right lateral pharyngeal wall, right tonsillar fossa, and hypopharynx were taken. Pathology has now returned and is the primary focus of today's visit.

Since the last visit, Ms. Osei-Bonsu reports gradual but meaningful improvement in her overall functional status. She is tolerating a soft mechanical diet orally and estimates that she is meeting approximately 75-80% of her caloric needs by mouth. PEG tube use has been significantly reduced: she is using the tube only overnight for supplemental nutrition and estimates fewer than 25% of daily calories are now coming through the PEG. She is no longer using opioid analgesia. She continues to experience notable xerostomia, which she describes as the most bothersome remaining symptom. She has resumed limited professional activity -- she has been visiting school part-time and is preparing to return to her music teaching position. No new neck masses palpated by the patient.
