- Magnesium: 1.8 mEq/L (low-normal; patient counseled on magnesium-rich dietary sources given ongoing risk from prior cisplatin)
- Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly reduced, consistent with ongoing nutritional recovery)
- Prealbumin: 18 mg/dL (slightly below normal; reflects suboptimal but improving nutritional status)
- TSH: 6.8 mIU/L (elevated, reference range 0.4-4.5 mIU/L) -- new finding consistent with radiation-induced hypothyroidism; this exceeds the expected result on her current levothyroxine dose of 75 mcg
- Free T4: 0.9 ng/dL (low-normal)
- LFTs: AST 22, ALT 18, Alkaline Phosphatase 78, Total Bilirubin 0.6 -- all within normal limits

Prior labs / pathology results reviewed today:
- Direct laryngoscopy pathology (approximately 4 weeks ago): Right base of tongue (3 fragments): extensive radiation effect with stromal fibrosis, vascular ectasia, and atypical stromal cells consistent with radiation change; NO VIABLE CARCINOMA IDENTIFIED. Right lateral pharyngeal wall: squamous mucosa with reactive changes, no malignancy. Right tonsillar fossa: benign lymphoid tissue with post-radiation fibrosis, no malignancy. Hypopharynx: squamous mucosa, no malignancy. CONCLUSION: Complete pathologic response to definitive chemoradiation.

Prior imaging reviewed:
- Restaging PET-CT (~10 weeks post-CRT): Complete metabolic response (SUVmax at primary 2.1, physiologic; right IIA node SUVmax 1.8, physiologic). No distant metastatic disease.
- MRI neck (~10 weeks post-CRT): Residual 0.9 x 0.7 cm area of decreased enhancement at right base of tongue, interpreted as post-treatment fibrosis vs. minimal residual tumor. Right IIA node 0.8 cm, no pathologic enhancement. No new adenopathy.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16) right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, T2N1M0, stage I (HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging), COMPLETE RESPONSE confirmed pathologically.
Today's visit provides the definitive confirmation we have been awaiting. The direct laryngoscopy biopsies returned showing NO VIABLE CARCINOMA -- extensive radiation effect with fibrosis but zero residual tumor cells in any biopsy specimen from the base of tongue or any sampled oropharyngeal/hypopharyngeal site. Combined with the prior PET-CT showing complete metabolic response and the MRI showing a markedly reduced 0.9 cm signal now confirmed to represent pure fibrosis, this represents a complete pathologic response by every criterion. I reviewed these results carefully with Ms. Osei-Bonsu today, emphasizing that this is excellent news. She was visibly emotional and expressed profound relief. I explained that in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, particularly in a never-smoker, a complete response to definitive chemoradiation at the 3-6 month post-treatment evaluation is associated with a very favorable long-term prognosis -- estimated 5-year locoregional control rates on the order of 85-90% in this favorable subgroup. I also explained that the residual sub-centimeter MRI signal that prompted the biopsy has now been definitively characterized as benign post-radiation fibrosis, which is reassuring and not unexpected given the intensity of radiation delivered to the primary site.

The primary driver of ongoing surveillance going forward is detection of late local recurrence (uncommon beyond 18-24 months in HPV-positive OPSCC), second primary tumors (particularly given HPV exposure), and late treatment toxicity management. No further oncologic therapy is indicated at this time.

2. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism -- NEW DIAGNOSIS
TSH today is 6.8 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. This is above the upper limit of normal and represents subclinical to overt hypothyroidism likely attributable to radiation injury to the thyroid gland, which is a well-recognized late complication of neck irradiation (particularly radiation fields covering the low neck). Her free T4 is low-normal at 0.9 ng/dL. Her prior TSH was normal prior to treatment on her maintenance levothyroxine dose of 75 mcg. The symptoms she described (fatigue, mild constipation) are consistent with undertreated hypothyroidism. I am increasing her levothyroxine dose from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily, effective today. TSH to be rechecked in 6-8 weeks. She was counseled to take levothyroxine on an empty stomach, at least 30-60 minutes before food and other medications. She understands that lifelong thyroid replacement is likely given radiation-induced thyroid damage.

3. Post-treatment dysphagia and nutritional recovery -- improving
Ms. Osei-Bonsu has made meaningful progress in oral rehabilitation over the past 3 months. She is now meeting approximately 75-80% of caloric needs orally on a soft mechanical diet, with PEG tube providing only supplemental overnight nutrition (estimated <25% of daily calories). Her weight is 68 kg, up approximately 2 kg from nadir but still 9 kg below her pre-treatment weight of 77 kg. Albumin and prealbumin remain mildly depressed, reflecting continued nutritional recovery. Dietetics was contacted today (telephone consultation) and agrees that oral intake is adequate to proceed with PEG removal planning when oral caloric intake consistently exceeds 1800-2000 kcal/day for 1-2 weeks. She will be seen by speech-language pathology next week for formal FEES reassessment to evaluate swallowing safety and guide dietary texture advancement. If the FEES is reassuring and oral intake targets are met over the next 4-6 weeks, we will plan PEG removal with gastroenterology. The patient is strongly motivated to have the PEG removed, which I view as a positive indicator of her functional recovery.

4. Persistent xerostomia -- grade 1-2
Continued significant complaint. Saliva production remains markedly reduced compared to pre-treatment baseline. She is using Biotene and frequent water sips with good behavioral coping. I discussed the option of initiating pilocarpine (a muscarinic agonist, sialagogue) for xerostomia, explaining that it may provide modest but meaningful benefit for some patients. She agreed to try pilocarpine 5 mg three times daily with meals, with a goal of increasing salivary flow. Common side effects (diaphoresis, flushing, rhinorrhea, urinary frequency) reviewed. She will trial for 4-6 weeks and report back. Dental hygiene counseled extensively: daily fluoride trays, xylitol gum/lozenges, and bi-annual dental care are strongly recommended given the risk of radiation caries. She has an appointment with her dentist next month.

5. Radiation-induced trismus -- mild, improving with therapy
Mouth opening measured at approximately 3.7-4.0 cm today, mildly below her estimated pre-treatment baseline of approximately 4.5 cm. She has been performing TheraBite jaw-stretching exercises at home and reports gradual improvement. I encouraged her to continue daily jaw stretching exercises, ideally 3-4 sets of 20 repetitions per day. No intervention beyond physical therapy is needed at this time. Physical medicine and rehabilitation consult to be considered if trismus worsens or does not continue to improve.

6. Mild anemia
Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL, normocytic. This is improved from the post-treatment nadir of approximately 9.8 g/dL and is consistent with slow recovery from combined chemoradiation-related bone marrow suppression and nutritional deficiency. Iron studies and B12/folate not checked today but were recently within normal limits at the 3-month visit; will recheck at next visit if hemoglobin does not continue trending up. No transfusion indicated. She is on a daily multivitamin.

7. Mild hypomagnesemia (1.8 mEq/L)
Persistent mild hypomagnesemia, a recognized late sequela of cisplatin therapy (cisplatin causes renal tubular magnesium wasting that can persist for months to years after treatment). Creatinine is now back to near-baseline (0.9), so renal function is recovering. Oral magnesium supplementation (magnesium oxide 400 mg daily) initiated, with instruction on GI tolerance (loose stools at higher doses). Will recheck at next visit.

---

PLAN

1. Complete pathologic response confirmed -- excellent news. No additional oncologic therapy needed. Surveillance plan per NCCN HPV-positive OPSCC guidelines:
   - Clinical exam (flexible nasopharyngoscopy included) every 1-3 months for the first year post-treatment completion; we are now at 6 months, next scheduled visit in 3 months (9 months post-treatment)
   - Restaging imaging: PET-CT planned at 9-12 months post-treatment (next major imaging checkpoint)
   - Annual thyroid function tests indefinitely (radiation-induced hypothyroidism now documented)
   - Dental follow-up every 6 months with fluoride treatment
   - Continue speech-language pathology quarterly

2. Increase levothyroxine from 75 mcg to 100 mcg daily for radiation-induced hypothyroidism. Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks (can be done by her primary care physician).

3. PEG tube removal planning: FEES (functional endoscopic evaluation of swallowing) with SLP next week. If swallowing is safe and oral intake targets maintained (>1800-2000 kcal/day orally, weight stable), proceed with PEG removal by gastroenterology in 4-6 weeks.

4. Initiate pilocarpine 5 mg TID with meals for xerostomia. Re-evaluate benefit/tolerability at next visit.

5. Initiate magnesium oxide 400 mg daily for hypomagnesemia. Recheck Mg at next visit.

6. Continue TheraBite jaw exercises for mild trismus.

7. Nutritional follow-up with dietetics at next visit to reassess caloric intake and guide transition to full oral diet.

8. Audiology follow-up in 3 months to assess for cisplatin-related ototoxicity (high-frequency SNHL monitoring).

9. Skin/dermatology: Post-radiation neck skin hyperpigmentation noted; patient counseled on sun protection (SPF 50+ to neck), moisturization. No intervention needed at this time.

10. Psychosocial support: Patient continues to cope well overall. Social work and/or survivorship counseling available if needed. Referred to the institutional cancer survivorship program for comprehensive late-effects monitoring.

Total visit time: approximately 50 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling, discussion of results, and care coordination.

Return to clinic: 3 months (9 months post-treatment completion), with PET-CT to be scheduled for that time point.

COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Specimen A (Right Base of Tongue Biopsy):
The biopsy fragments demonstrate an infiltrating squamous cell carcinoma arising from the oropharyngeal mucosa of the right base of tongue. The tumor is moderately differentiated (AJCC/CAP grade 2) and exhibits architectural features including irregular nests and sheets of neoplastic squamous cells with central keratinization (keratin pearl formation), intercellular bridge formation (desmosomes), and individual cell dyskeratosis. Tumor cells display enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei with prominent nucleoli, coarse chromatin, and moderate-to-abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm. Mitotic figures, including atypical forms, are readily identified. There is a desmoplastic stromal reaction with admixed chronic inflammatory infiltrate, including a prominent tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) component at the tumor-stromal interface, which is characteristic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC. No surface epithelial dysplasia is identified in the adjacent mucosa (non-keratinizing surface overlying the invasive carcinoma). Assessment of surgical margins is not applicable in the context of diagnostic biopsy.

The immunohistochemical profile is consistent with squamous cell differentiation: CK5/6 strongly positive, p63 strongly positive in tumor nuclei, CK7 negative. This profile, combined with the morphology, confirms squamous cell lineage.

p16 immunohistochemistry demonstrates block-like, diffuse, strong nuclear and cytoplasmic staining in greater than 75% of tumor cells (score 3+), meeting the College of American Pathologists (CAP) and ISCAP criteria for p16 positivity as a surrogate marker of transcriptionally active high-risk HPV infection in oropharyngeal SCC.

HPV RNA chromogenic in situ hybridization (CISH), performed using a high-risk HPV RNA probe cocktail (targeting HPV types 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 51, 52, 56, 58, 66), demonstrates punctate and diffuse dot-pattern intranuclear RNA signal in the majority of tumor cells, consistent with transcriptionally active high-risk HPV infection. Genotype-specific follow-up testing confirms HPV type 16 RNA expression.

Ki-67 (MIB-1) proliferation index: approximately 65% of tumor nuclei positive, reflecting the high proliferative activity characteristic of HPV-driven oropharyngeal SCC.

EBER in situ hybridization: NEGATIVE (performed to exclude nasopharyngeal carcinoma with EBV association).

Taken together, the histomorphologic, immunohistochemical, and in situ hybridization findings are diagnostic of HPV-associated (p16-positive, HPV type 16 RNA-positive) invasive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, moderately differentiated. This is consistent with the clinically and radiographically identified primary oropharyngeal tumor.

Specimen B (Right Level II Cervical Lymph Node, Fine Needle Aspiration):
Direct smears are highly cellular and demonstrate dispersed and loosely cohesive clusters of atypical epithelial cells with irregular nuclear contours, prominent nucleoli, and moderate cytoplasm, set against a background of lymphocytes and necrotic debris, consistent with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in a lymph node. Cell block preparations confirm the presence of malignant squamous cells. p16 immunohistochemistry performed on the cell block shows strong, diffuse, block-like nuclear and cytoplasmic positivity in the tumor cells, identical in pattern to the primary biopsy. These findings confirm metastatic involvement of the right level II cervical lymph node by p16-positive squamous cell carcinoma, concordant with the right base of tongue primary.

NOTE: Additional molecular testing (NGS comprehensive panel, PD-L1 CPS by IHC) has been submitted separately and results will be reported as an addendum.

WHOLE-BODY PET-CT WITH 18F-FDG
SURVEILLANCE STUDY — OROPHARYNGEAL SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, POST-DEFINITIVE CHEMORADIATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female with a history of p16-positive (HPV-associated) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition), treated with definitive concurrent intensity-modulated radiation therapy (70 Gy in 35 fractions to the high-risk CTV via SIB technique) and weekly cisplatin (6 of 7 planned cycles administered; cumulative dose ~414 mg/m2). Treatment was completed approximately 9 months prior to today's examination. Post-treatment restaging at 10 weeks showed complete metabolic response on PET-CT with no FDG-avid disease, and subsequent direct laryngoscopy with biopsy approximately 5 months post-treatment confirmed complete pathologic response. The patient has undergone PEG tube removal and has been tolerating a soft mechanical diet. This examination is obtained for routine surveillance at the 9–12 month post-treatment imaging interval per institutional protocol.

TECHNIQUE:
Following an intravenous injection of approximately 12.6 mCi of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose, the patient was placed in a quiet, dimly lit room with an intravenous line in place for a mandatory uptake period of 60 minutes. Blood glucose at time of injection was 84 mg/dL (within acceptable range). Low-dose non-contrast CT was acquired from vertex to mid-thigh for attenuation correction and anatomic localization, followed by PET acquisition in 3D mode. Dedicated diagnostic-quality CT of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL, 100 mL, injected at 3 mL/sec with 60-second delay) was obtained in the same session. Images were reconstructed with ordered-subsets expectation maximization (OSEM) algorithm, 3 iterations, 24 subsets, with time-of-flight correction. Fusion PET-CT images were reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes on dedicated nuclear medicine workstation.

COMPARISON:
Prior PET-CT performed approximately 10 weeks post-chemoradiation completion (complete metabolic response at that time). Baseline pre-treatment PET-CT (right base of tongue SUVmax 14.2, right level IIA node SUVmax 9.8).

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK — PRIMARY SITE:
The right base of tongue demonstrates a small area of mildly increased soft tissue density on CT corresponding to the previously described region of post-treatment fibrosis, stable in size and morphology compared to the 10-week post-treatment PET-CT. On PET images, this region shows a low level of FDG uptake measuring SUVmax 1.9, which is symmetric with activity in the uninvolved contralateral base of tongue and well within the range expected for physiologic mucosal activity in the setting of prior radiation. There is no focal area of metabolically active tissue that would suggest viable residual or recurrent tumor at the primary site. The treated mucosa of the oropharynx demonstrates diffusely reduced FDG avidity compared to pre-treatment imaging, consistent with the expected physiologic attenuation following high-dose radiation to this region.

HEAD AND NECK — REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
The previously FDG-avid right level IIA lymph node — the site of biopsy-proven nodal metastasis at diagnosis — is now markedly reduced in size, measuring approximately 0.8 cm in short-axis dimension on CT. No pathologic FDG activity is associated with this node; background uptake of SUVmax 1.6 is recorded, indistinguishable from vascular and soft tissue background activity. No other right-sided cervical lymph nodes demonstrate pathologic enlargement or metabolic activity. The bilateral levels IB, IIA, IIB, III, IV, and V chains are surveyed with no nodes exhibiting either morphologic or metabolic features concerning for malignancy. The left neck is unremarkable. Bilateral parotid glands are mildly atrophic, consistent with the expected sequela of scatter radiation dose during IMRT, with no discrete FDG-avid nodules. No retropharyngeal, parapharyngeal, or submandibular pathologic adenopathy identified.

HEAD AND NECK — OTHER STRUCTURES:
The salivary glands bilaterally are mildly diminished in volume compared to pre-treatment imaging, consistent with radiation-induced atrophy. No discrete mass or FDG-avid lesion within the parotid, submandibular, or sublingual glands bilaterally. The nasopharynx, hypopharynx, larynx, and oral cavity are unremarkable on both CT and PET without evidence of new mucosal lesion or increased metabolic activity. The thyroid gland is homogeneous in attenuation on CT without discrete nodule; mild diffuse decreased uptake within the thyroid gland is noted, consistent with radiation scatter effect to the thyroid bed — no focal FDG-avid thyroid nodule identified. Bony structures of the skull base, mandible, and cervical spine demonstrate normal CT density and no abnormal FDG uptake.

CHEST:
The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, consolidations, or pleural effusions are present. Mediastinal and hilar contours are within normal limits. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy by size criteria or metabolic activity. No FDG-avid intrathoracic lesion identified. The heart demonstrates normal physiologic myocardial FDG distribution. There is no pericardial effusion. The visualized osseous structures of the thorax including ribs, sternum, clavicles, and thoracic spine show no lytic or sclerotic lesion and no abnormal metabolic activity.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
The liver parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation and demonstrates uniform physiologic FDG uptake without discrete focal lesion. No hepatic lesions identified. The spleen is normal in size and without FDG-avid lesion. The pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable bilaterally. The kidneys demonstrate symmetric physiologic urinary tracer excretion without focal cortical lesion. No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. Bowel loops demonstrate physiologic FDG activity without discrete focus of elevated uptake. The uterus and adnexa are not separately identified; no suspicious pelvic mass or lymphadenopathy. There is no ascites. The bladder contains physiologic tracer. No bony lesion or abnormal skeletal FDG uptake in the lumbar spine, sacrum, pelvis, or proximal femora.

IMPRESSION:

1. No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue primary site. The focal area of mildly increased uptake at the treated base of tongue (SUVmax 1.9) is symmetric with contralateral physiologic mucosal activity and is not above the threshold for concern; appearance is stable and consistent with post-radiation mucosal and soft tissue changes.

2. No pathologic cervical lymphadenopathy on either morphologic or metabolic assessment. The previously positive right level IIA node is now markedly reduced in size without FDG activity, consistent with durable complete nodal response.

3. No distant metastatic disease identified in the thorax, abdomen, or pelvis.

4. Incidental mildly diffuse reduction in thyroid FDG uptake and mild atrophic changes in bilateral parotid glands, both consistent with post-radiation sequelae. Clinical correlation with thyroid function tests is advised on an ongoing basis.

5. Overall, these findings are consistent with a sustained, complete metabolic response at approximately 9 months following definitive concurrent chemoradiation for HPV-positive right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma. No evidence of locoregional recurrence or new distant metastatic disease.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to do extremely well at her 18-month surveillance visit. She has no clinical, endoscopic, or radiographic evidence of disease recurrence. The MRI of the neck today is reassuring — the fibrotic area at the right base of tongue has continued to shrink and shows no enhancement, which is highly consistent with maturing post-radiation fibrosis rather than residual or recurrent tumor. The flexible nasopharyngoscopy performed in clinic today shows smooth, stable post-radiation fibrotic changes at the base of tongue with no mucosal irregularity, friability, or mass effect to suggest recurrence.

I discussed with Ms. Osei-Bonsu that reaching 18 months of disease-free status following definitive treatment for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC is a meaningful and encouraging milestone. The biology of HPV-positive OPSCC — in contrast to HPV-negative disease — is characterized by excellent response rates to chemoradiation and favorable long-term outcomes in never-smokers with complete response, such as herself. The 5-year locoregional control and overall survival rates for patients with her profile (T2N1M0, HPV-positive, never-smoker, complete response by both imaging and biopsy) are in the range of 85-90%.

Plan:

PET-CT WITH 18F-FDG, WHOLE BODY
SURVEILLANCE IMAGING — 24-MONTH POST-TREATMENT CHECKPOINT

INDICATION:
Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 54-year-old female with a history of HPV-positive (p16+), high-risk HPV type 16, squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, staged T2N1M0 at diagnosis (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging; stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria). She was treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation — intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using a simultaneous integrated boost technique to 70 Gy at the high-risk target volume, delivered over 35 fractions, with concurrent weekly Platinol (cumulative dose 414 mg/m2; 83% of planned dose delivered). Treatment was completed approximately 24 months prior to this examination. She achieved a complete metabolic response on post-treatment PET-CT performed at approximately 10 weeks post-chemoradiation, which was confirmed by direct laryngoscopy and biopsy demonstrating complete pathologic response (no viable carcinoma). Subsequent surveillance imaging at 9 months demonstrated no evidence of recurrent disease, and MRI neck at 18 months was similarly reassuring with stable post-treatment fibrotic changes at the primary site. This examination is being performed as a 24-month surveillance PET-CT per institutional head and neck oncology surveillance protocol for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.

Never-smoker. Known late treatment effects include grade 1 xerostomia and mild high-frequency sensorineural hearing loss. Clinically doing well, ECOG performance status 0.

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body PET-CT was performed following intravenous administration of 10.8 mCi (399 MBq) of 18F-FDG. The patient fasted for greater than 6 hours prior to injection. Pre-injection serum glucose was 91 mg/dL. Uptake phase was 62 minutes. Diagnostic-quality CT was acquired from the vertex of the skull through the mid-thighs with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL at 3 mL/sec). Images were reconstructed using time-of-flight PET with iterative reconstruction and reviewed in attenuation-corrected and non-attenuation-corrected formats. SUV calculations utilize lean body mass (SUVlbm) and maximum pixel SUV (SUVmax). A low-dose CT component was also available for attenuation correction and anatomic coregistration.

Ms. Osei-Bonsu has achieved and maintained a complete response to definitive chemoradiation for her stage III (HPV-positive oropharyngeal stage I) base of tongue SCC. Today's PET-CT at the 24-month mark is entirely reassuring — no FDG-avid disease anywhere. This is a highly significant milestone. In the context of HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, the vast majority of recurrences, if they occur, happen within the first 24 months. The biology of HPV-driven OPSCC confers a much more favorable prognosis compared to HPV-negative disease, and sustained complete metabolic response at 2 years is strongly predictive of long-term cure. We estimate her 5-year locoregional control probability at approximately 85-90% and 5-year overall survival at approximately 90%, reflecting the combination of complete response, HPV-positive status, never-smoker history, and young age at diagnosis.

I counseled the patient extensively on the significance of this milestone. She was understandably relieved and emotional during this discussion. I reinforced that while we will continue surveillance, we are highly optimistic about her long-term outcome. Late recurrences beyond 24 months do occur — particularly distant metastases in HPV-positive OPSCC, which can occasionally manifest later than local recurrences — and she should continue to be vigilant about new symptoms. However, the risk decreases substantially at this point.

Surveillance plan going forward:
- Clinical exam: every 6 months (decreasing from current every 4-6 months schedule)
- Imaging: annual surveillance will be transitioned to clinical-exam-driven; we will not routinely scan annually at this point given her excellent status and the low incremental yield of annual PET-CT beyond 2 years in this setting. We will obtain imaging if clinical exam raises any concern or if new symptoms develop. This approach is consistent with NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC in complete responders.
- Annual thyroid ultrasound will continue for radiation-induced thyroid cancer screening.
- Carotid artery Doppler: repeat in 12 months (next due at approximately 42 months post-radiation).

2. LATE RADIATION TOXICITIES — ONGOING MANAGEMENT:

Xerostomia (grade 1, stable): Patient continues pilocarpine 5 mg BID (tapered from TID at 18 months as noted in prior visit). She is managing well with Biotene gel and regular water intake. No significant change. We will continue current management. If symptoms worsen, consider increasing pilocarpine back to TID or evaluating for acupuncture as a complementary approach for xerostomia (evidence is modest but some patients report benefit).

Dysphagia (grade 1, improved): The patient now tolerates a regular diet with only minor modifications — she avoids extremely dry or sticky foods. This represents significant improvement from the post-treatment period when she required PEG tube feeding. No formal FEES or modified barium swallow is indicated at this time given her subjective and functional improvement. She will continue quarterly speech-language pathology check-ins, transitioning to twice-yearly going forward.

Trismus (resolved): Mouth opening now 4.0-4.1 cm, close to estimated baseline of 4.5 cm. Patient should continue TheraBite exercises as maintenance, though intensity can be reduced now that she has achieved near-normal range of motion.

Sensorineural hearing loss (grade 1, stable): Mild high-frequency SNHL at 4000-8000 Hz bilaterally on prior audiogram, attributed to cisplatin ototoxicity. Not causing functional difficulty. Annual audiologic assessment to monitor for progression.

Hypothyroidism (radiation-induced): Well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH 1.6 mIU/L today. Continue current dose. Annual thyroid function testing.

Cardiovascular risk — radiation-induced carotid artery changes: Low-dose atorvastatin 10 mg daily was initiated at the 30-month mark (approximately 6 months ago) for cardiovascular risk reduction. LDL now 88 mg/dL on statin, which is a good response. No carotid symptoms. Carotid Doppler scheduled at next annual visit. Lifestyle counseling reinforced (heart-healthy diet, physical activity).

As noted in the prior clinic note, genetics counseling reclassified the BRCA2 p.D2723H variant as likely benign based on updated population databases and functional evidence. No additional BRCA-directed screening or testing is recommended for the patient or her family members at this time. The patient was re-counseled today and is comfortable with this conclusion. She will continue age-appropriate routine cancer screening (mammography, cervical cytology as applicable).

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
See above — well-managed. Continue levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily.

- Dental hygiene: reinforced importance of meticulous oral hygiene and fluoride treatments to prevent dental caries in the setting of chronic xerostomia. She is followed by her dentist every 6 months with prescription fluoride rinse (1.1% NaF gel, nightly).
- Nutritional status: weight 71 kg, stable. BMI 24.1. Dietetics follow-up annually.

PET-CT SCAN WITH 18F-FDG (WHOLE BODY WITH DEDICATED NECK)

EXAMINATION: PET-CT, 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose, whole body with dedicated head and neck protocol
INDICATION: 54-year-old female with history of HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma, right base of tongue (T2N1M0 by conventional AJCC 8th edition staging; stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging criteria), treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy and intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT), completed approximately 24 months prior. Imaging obtained for two-year post-treatment oncologic surveillance.
COMPARISON: Prior surveillance PET-CT performed approximately 9 months post-treatment; prior surveillance MRI neck performed approximately 18 months post-treatment; prior restaging PET-CT performed approximately 10 weeks post-treatment.

---

TECHNIQUE: Following intravenous injection of 10.2 mCi of 18F-FDG, whole-body PET imaging was performed from the vertex of the skull through the proximal thighs after an uptake period of 60 minutes. Blood glucose at the time of injection was 87 mg/dL. Low-dose CT images were acquired for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. Dedicated high-resolution CT images of the neck were obtained with IV contrast for anatomic characterization of the head and neck region.

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK -- PRIMARY SITE:
The right base of tongue demonstrates no abnormal FDG activity. The maximum standardized uptake value (SUVmax) in this region measures 1.7, which is well within the range of normal background mucosal and lymphoid tissue activity for this anatomic site. No focal, asymmetric, or pathologically elevated metabolic activity is identified at the primary tumor bed. There is subtle mucosal thickening and mild T2 signal heterogeneity in the right base of tongue on the fused images, appearing stable to slightly improved compared to prior examinations and consistent with post-radiation fibrotic change. No discrete enhancing mass. The right vallecula, bilateral tonsillar fossae, soft palate, posterior pharyngeal wall, and hypopharynx are unremarkable without focal FDG uptake above background. The left base of tongue demonstrates symmetric physiologic activity (SUVmax 1.6). The larynx, including the epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, and true and false vocal cords, are unremarkable without hypermetabolic activity.

REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
The previously documented right level IIA lymph node, which had demonstrated avid FDG uptake at staging (SUVmax 9.8), is no longer metabolically active. A small residual node in this location measures approximately 0.6 cm in short axis and demonstrates an SUVmax of 1.4, entirely in keeping with a small benign or fibrotic lymph node. No internal necrosis. No pathologic FDG activity is identified within the right or left cervical nodal chains at any level (levels I-VI bilaterally). No retropharyngeal, parapharyngeal, or posterior cervical triangle nodal hypermetabolism. No supraclavicular nodal FDG uptake bilaterally. Overall, the bilateral cervical nodal stations are unremarkable.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The parotid glands appear mildly reduced in volume bilaterally, somewhat more pronounced on the right, consistent with expected post-radiation atrophy and fatty replacement, a finding that has been progressive compared to pre-treatment examinations. No focal FDG-avid lesions within the parotid or submandibular glands. The sublingual glands are unremarkable. No suspicious nodules or calculi.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland demonstrates homogeneous, symmetric, low-level background uptake without focal FDG-avid nodules or masses (thyroid SUVmax 1.3). The gland is mildly reduced in volume compared to earlier imaging, a well-recognized post-radiation change following neck irradiation. No dominant nodule, calcification, or cystic change identifiable on the CT component. No thyroid hypermetabolism. Findings are stable in comparison to prior studies.

CHEST:
The lungs are clear throughout. There are no pulmonary nodules, masses, consolidation, or pleural effusions. Mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes are within normal size limits and show no significant FDG uptake. No mediastinal mass. The heart appears morphologically normal. There is no pericardial effusion. Thoracic aorta is unremarkable. No axillary adenopathy. The osseous structures of the thorax demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions and no FDG-avid bone marrow abnormalities.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
The liver is normal in size and homogeneous in density without focal lesions. No hepatic FDG uptake beyond background (liver SUVmax used as reference: 2.4). The spleen is within normal size limits without focal lesions or hypermetabolism. The pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. No abdominal or retroperitoneal adenopathy. Normal physiologic FDG activity is seen in the bowel and genitourinary collecting systems, without discrete focal lesions. No pelvic masses. No pelvic or inguinal adenopathy of concern. The uterus and adnexa are unremarkable.

SKELETAL SURVEY:
No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions. No FDG-avid bone lesions suggesting metastatic involvement. There is mild degenerative change in the lumbar spine and bilateral facet joints, without associated hypermetabolism. The cervical vertebrae and skull base demonstrate no worrisome osseous or FDG-avid findings. No pathologic fracture.

SOFT TISSUES:
No soft tissue masses or FDG-avid subcutaneous lesions. Mild post-radiation soft tissue changes in the right neck are noted on CT (mild subcutaneous thickening, reticular density in the right neck soft tissues), stable compared to prior examinations and consistent with expected post-radiation fibrosis. No lymphedema or significant soft tissue swelling.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue primary tumor site. The SUVmax in this region (1.7) is at or below physiologic background, and no metabolically active lesion is identified. This is consistent with durable complete metabolic response at the primary site, now confirmed across multiple post-treatment imaging time points.

2. The previously involved right level IIA lymph node has remained metabolically quiescent, with a small residual fibrotic-appearing node of 0.6 cm and SUVmax 1.4. No locoregional nodal recurrence identified.

3. No evidence of distant metastatic disease in the chest, abdomen, pelvis, or skeleton.

4. No FDG-avid thyroid nodule. Thyroid volume mildly reduced, in keeping with post-radiation changes; clinical correlation with thyroid function tests is recommended as per standard survivorship care.

5. Post-radiation atrophic changes in the bilateral parotid glands, right greater than left, and soft tissue post-radiation fibrosis in the right neck -- all stable and consistent with expected late sequelae of prior neck irradiation.

Overall, this two-year post-treatment surveillance PET-CT demonstrates no radiographic or metabolic evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic spread. These findings are highly reassuring in the context of HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma treated with definitive chemoradiation.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 54-year-old West African-descent female, never-smoker, with a history of HPV-positive (p16+, HPV-16) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition stage III conventional; HPV-positive oropharyngeal stage I), who presents today for routine survivorship follow-up at 30 months following completion of definitive concurrent cisplatin-based chemoradiation. She reports no new symptoms and comes in for scheduled surveillance.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu was initially diagnosed approximately two and a half years ago with a 3.2 cm right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma with a single right level IIA metastatic lymph node (2.1 cm) following a 3-month history of progressive right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. She is a never-smoker. Tumor was confirmed p16 strongly positive (>75% of cells, block staining), and HPV RNA in situ hybridization confirmed high-risk HPV type 16 expression. NGS at diagnosis demonstrated a PIK3CA E545K activating mutation, TERT promoter C228T mutation, CCND1 amplification, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion, among other alterations. PD-L1 CPS was 12. MSI-stable, TMB 6.2 mutations/Mb.

She was treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation: IMRT using a simultaneous integrated boost technique delivering 70 Gy/35 fractions to the high-risk CTV (primary and right IIA node), 63 Gy to intermediate-risk elective bilateral neck volumes, and 56 Gy to low-risk elective nodes, using VMAT technique with daily CBCT guidance. She received concurrent weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2; 6 of 7 planned cycles were delivered (cumulative dose 414 mg/m2, approximately 83% of planned, representing adequate systemic therapy). Her week 5 cisplatin dose was held for grade 2 nephrotoxicity and grade 2 thrombocytopenia.

Treatment course was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis during week 4 requiring a 3-day inpatient admission for IV hydration, pain management (patient-controlled analgesia with morphine, transitioned to extended-release oxycodone), and initiation of PEG tube feeds (Vital 1.5 at 75 mL/hr). She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the right neck, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 1 fatigue during treatment. She lost approximately 9 kg (12% body weight) by the end of treatment.

Post-treatment course has been excellent. Restaging PET-CT at 10 weeks post-treatment showed complete metabolic response (SUVmax at base of tongue 2.1, right level IIA node resolved with no FDG uptake); MRI confirmed near-complete anatomic response. Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of the right base of tongue at 5 months post-treatment showed no viable carcinoma, confirming complete pathologic response. PEG tube was removed at approximately 7 months post-treatment after she demonstrated adequate oral caloric intake. Surveillance PET-CT at 9 months showed no evidence of recurrence. At her 12-month visit she had returned to full-time work. MRI neck at 18 months was stable with no radiographic evidence of recurrent disease. PET-CT at 24 months confirmed no FDG-avid disease at the primary site or regional nodes.

Today she presents at 30 months post-completion of chemoradiation. She reports no new neck masses, no worsening throat pain or odynophagia, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea, no new oral lesions, no ear pain, and no new neurologic symptoms. She denies otalgia, trismus, or significant jaw dysfunction. She does note persistent mild dry mouth, which she manages well with Biotene and pilocarpine. She denies any new constitutional symptoms: no fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. She reports she has been active at work as a high school music teacher and has resumed essentially her full pre-illness routine.

Mild taste alteration persists but has continued to improve over time. No otalgia. Mild, stable high-frequency hearing loss in both ears, previously noted on audiogram -- no new subjective worsening. Gastrointestinal: Tolerating oral diet well -- currently on a soft-to-regular diet. No dysphagia for liquids. Mild dysphagia for dry or thick solid foods (avoids bread, crackers, dry meats), but otherwise managing well. No nausea, no vomiting. PEG tube removed over a year ago; site healed without complication.

IMAGING REVIEW

The most recent imaging study available for review is the surveillance PET-CT performed at 24 months post-chemoradiation (2-year mark). At that time, there was no FDG-avid disease at the right base of tongue (SUVmax 1.7, physiologic range), no pathologic cervical adenopathy, and no distant metastases. The study was read as complete ongoing metabolic response with no evidence of locoregional recurrence or distant disease.

The most recent MRI of the neck was performed at 18 months, showing stable post-treatment fibrotic changes at the right base of tongue (0.5 cm, no enhancement), no new mucosal lesions, and no pathologic adenopathy.

No new cross-sectional imaging has been obtained today. Given the clinical examination, symptom review, and prior imaging stability through 24 months, no urgent imaging is indicated at this visit. Surveillance imaging will be discussed in the assessment and plan section.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HPV-POSITIVE RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE SCC, T2N1M0 (AJCC 8TH EDITION, STAGE I BY HPV-POSITIVE OROPHARYNGEAL CRITERIA), STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CISPLATIN AND IMRT -- COMPLETE RESPONSE SUSTAINED, NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE AT 30 MONTHS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu continues to do extremely well at 30 months following completion of her definitive chemoradiation. She achieved a complete metabolic response on restaging PET-CT at 10 weeks, confirmed by direct laryngoscopy biopsy showing complete pathologic response at 5 months, and has had no clinical, radiographic, or biochemical evidence of disease recurrence through the 24-month surveillance PET-CT and 18-month MRI neck. Today's clinical examination is reassuring: no palpable lymphadenopathy, no new symptoms, and flexible nasopharyngoscopy shows stable post-radiation fibrotic changes at the right base of tongue without any visible lesion, exophytic mass, or mucosal irregularity suspicious for recurrence.

This 30-month milestone is clinically meaningful. In HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC treated with definitive chemoradiation, the vast majority of recurrences occur within the first 24 months. Her never-smoker status, HPV-positive histology, complete metabolic and pathologic response, and absence of adverse clinical features confer a favorable prognosis, with estimated 5-year disease-specific survival in the range of 85-90%. The probability of late recurrence (beyond 30 months) in her clinical context is quite low.

Surveillance plan: Given that she is now 30 months post-treatment and has been stable through the 24-month imaging checkpoint, we will continue with reduced-frequency surveillance. Per our practice and NCCN-aligned guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC:
- Clinical examination (including flexible nasopharyngoscopy): every 4-6 months.
- Annual MRI neck: next MRI neck to be scheduled at 36 months post-treatment.
- Annual PET-CT is not routinely recommended beyond 24 months given her complete response and HPV-positive low-risk biology; we will reserve repeat PET-CT for any clinically concerning findings or symptoms.
- Annual thyroid function testing and thyroid ultrasound: continued as part of radiation-induced thyroid disease surveillance.
- Annual carotid artery Doppler: continued given radiation exposure to cervical vasculature.
- Annual audiology evaluation: continued for monitoring of cisplatin-related SNHL.

2. RADIATION-INDUCED CAROTID ARTERY DISEASE -- EARLY INTIMAL-MEDIAL THICKENING, STATIN INITIATED

Carotid Doppler today shows mild right-sided intimal-medial thickening (0.9 mm) in the radiation field without hemodynamically significant stenosis (<30% bilaterally). This is a recognized late effect of neck irradiation and correlates with her radiation exposure to the right carotid vasculature. She was started on atorvastatin 10 mg daily at her 24-month visit for cardiovascular risk reduction in the setting of radiation-associated carotid disease, per current guidelines. LDL today is 118 mg/dL on statin (baseline); will recheck fasting lipids in 3 months to assess treatment response. Target LDL in this setting: <100 mg/dL given the radiation-associated vascular risk. Will reassess statin dose at next visit.
- Continue atorvastatin 10 mg daily.
- Annual carotid Doppler: next study in 12 months.
- Counseled on stroke awareness and when to seek emergency evaluation.
- Blood pressure well-controlled (122/76 today).

3. LATE RADIATION TOXICITIES -- XEROSTOMIA, SENSORINEURAL HEARING LOSS, MILD RESIDUAL DYSPHAGIA

a. Xerostomia (grade 1, persistent but improving): She continues on pilocarpine, which was tapered to 5 mg BID at her 24-month visit. She reports adequate symptom management with pilocarpine and Biotene. No further dose escalation at this time. Will continue current regimen. Counseled to maintain excellent dental hygiene and continue fluoride trays given radiation-related increased risk of dental caries.

b. Sensorineural hearing loss (grade 1, high-frequency, bilateral, stable): Audiogram today is unchanged from 12 months, with mild bilateral SNHL at 4000-8000 Hz consistent with cisplatin ototoxicity. No intervention warranted at this level. Annual audiologic monitoring will continue. If she develops hearing aids-qualifying loss, audiologic amplification to be considered at that time. No ototoxic medications added.

c. Dysphagia (mild residual, grade 1): Tolerating regular diet with minor modifications (avoids dry, dense foods). Has been discharged from formal speech-language pathology follow-up but encouraged to continue home jaw exercises and maintain dietary variety. No frank aspiration reported. If dysphagia worsens, referral to SLP for repeat FEES evaluation.

d. Trismus (resolved): Mouth opening now 4.2 cm, improved from nadir of 3.8 cm. Essentially resolved. TheraBite home exercises were helpful; encouraged to continue periodic jaw-stretching maintenance.

e. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism: Stable on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH 1.8 mIU/L today. Annual thyroid function checks and thyroid ultrasound (negative today, no nodules). Continue current levothyroxine dose.

4. BRCA2 p.D2723H VARIANT -- RECLASSIFIED AS LIKELY BENIGN

The genetics counseling team has reclassified the BRCA2 p.D2723H germline variant of uncertain significance (originally identified on somatic tumor NGS with allele frequency 48%, suggesting germline carrier status) as likely benign based on current evidence. This is reassuring. No BRCA2-related cancer surveillance or risk-reduction interventions are warranted at this time. Family member cascade testing for this specific variant is not indicated given reclassification.

5. PREVENTIVE CARE AND COMORBIDITIES

- Hypothyroidism (well-controlled, as above).
- Cardiovascular risk reduction: statin initiated, BP well-controlled, no smoking history (never-smoker).
- Dental surveillance: Annual dental evaluations with radiation oncology awareness are recommended given prior neck radiation. Dental hygiene instructions reinforced.

- HPV vaccination: Not applicable (prior HPV exposure documented).

MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST — STAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION: 52-year-old female with newly diagnosed right base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma (p16-positive), p/w right-sided odynophagia and palpable right neck mass. Referred for staging MRI of the neck with and without gadolinium contrast.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the neck was performed on a 3.0 Tesla MRI system. Sequences obtained include axial and coronal T1-weighted, axial T2-weighted with fat saturation, axial and sagittal STIR, and axial/coronal T1-weighted post-contrast sequences with fat saturation following the uneventful intravenous administration of 15 mL gadolinium-based contrast agent (gadobutrol). Dedicated high-resolution small-FOV sequences through the oropharynx and tongue base were obtained. No contraindications to gadolinium administration. Images are of diagnostic quality.

---

FINDINGS:

PRIMARY TUMOR — OROPHARYNX / BASE OF TONGUE:
There is a lobular, heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue mass arising from the right base of tongue, measuring approximately 3.2 cm in the transverse dimension x 2.8 cm in the anteroposterior dimension x 2.5 cm in the craniocaudal dimension. The lesion demonstrates intermediate to slightly elevated T2 signal with avid post-contrast enhancement and focal internal areas of lower T2/T1 signal consistent with dense cellularity or early central necrosis. On T1 pre-contrast imaging, the mass is isointense to adjacent tongue musculature. The tumor is centered in the right glossotonsillar sulcus and base of tongue, with exophytic growth toward the oropharyngeal lumen. There is extension of the mass toward the right tonsillar fossa/peritonsillar soft tissues; however, the ipsilateral palatine tonsil itself is not unambiguously invaded. The deep extension of the mass does not appear to reach the extrinsic tongue musculature (genioglossus, hyoglossus) at the midline, and there is no definite crossing of the midline. Superiorly, the mass does not extend to the soft palate. Inferiorly, there is no involvement of the vallecula or the pre-epiglottic fat space; the fat within the pre-epiglottic space appears entirely preserved. There is no tumor extension into the sublingual space. No clear evidence of perineural spread along the lingual nerve, hypoglossal nerve, or glossopharyngeal nerve; the styloglossus and hyoglossus muscles appear preserved bilaterally. The floor of mouth and oral tongue are uninvolved.

RIGHT NECK — REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
There is a single enlarged lymph node in the right level IIA region (jugulodigastric/upper jugular chain), measuring 2.1 cm x 1.8 cm in axial dimensions. This node demonstrates a rounded contour with loss of the normal reniform hilar architecture and exhibits peripheral rim-enhancement on post-contrast sequences with a central region of T2 hypersignal and T1 hypointensity, morphologically consistent with central liquefactive necrosis — findings that are highly suspicious for metastatic involvement. There is no extracapsular extension of tumor identified on imaging; the fat plane between the node and the adjacent internal jugular vein appears preserved, and there is no frank invasion of the sternocleidomastoid muscle or adjacent vascular structures.

No additional ipsilateral cervical adenopathy is identified at levels IIB, III, IV, or V. The right submandibular (level IB) nodes are not enlarged (largest approximately 0.7 cm, oval, with preserved fatty hilum). No right level VI (paratracheal) adenopathy.

LEFT NECK — REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the left neck at levels I through V. The left jugulodigastric region contains a cluster of small oval nodes, the largest measuring 0.9 cm, with preserved fatty hila and benign morphology, most likely reactive. No contralateral adenopathy.

RETROPHARYNGEAL SPACE:
No retropharyngeal adenopathy identified. The retropharyngeal space is symmetric and clear bilaterally. No asymmetric soft tissue thickening or abnormal enhancement.

SKULL BASE AND MANDIBLE:
The visualized skull base, including the clivus, sphenoid bone, and posterior fossa, appears intact without evidence of osseous erosion or marrow replacement. The mandible is well-corticated bilaterally without evidence of invasion or periosteal reaction. There is no bone marrow edema along the lingual or inferior alveolar nerve course.

PAROTID AND SUBMANDIBULAR GLANDS:
Bilateral parotid glands are symmetric in size and signal without intrinsic mass or ductal dilation. Bilateral submandibular glands appear normal.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid gland is of normal size and signal. There are no discrete thyroid nodules identified on this non-dedicated examination.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
The bilateral common carotid arteries, internal carotid arteries, and internal jugular veins appear patent and without extrinsic compression from the primary tumor or nodal disease. The carotid space is patent bilaterally.

SOFT TISSUES / OTHER:
No abnormal signal in the prevertebral or paravertebral musculature. The nasopharynx appears symmetric without mass or asymmetric mucosal thickening. The hypopharynx and larynx, to the extent visualized on this examination, appear unremarkable; the true and false vocal cords appear symmetric without mass. Visualized brain on lower slices: no acute abnormality.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Right base of tongue mass, approximately 3.2 x 2.8 cm, with avid heterogeneous enhancement and lobular morphology, centered in the right glossotonsillar sulcus. The lesion extends toward the right tonsillar fossa but does not involve the vallecula, pre-epiglottic space, or cross the midline. No definite perineural invasion identified on imaging. Appearances are consistent with the known primary squamous cell carcinoma.

2. Single ipsilateral right level IIA lymph node, approximately 2.1 x 1.8 cm, with central necrosis and peripheral rim-enhancement, highly suspicious for metastatic involvement. No extracapsular extension apparent on imaging.

3. No contralateral cervical adenopathy. No retropharyngeal adenopathy.

4. No evidence of bony involvement of the mandible or skull base.

5. Overall findings are consistent with locoregional disease confined to the right oropharynx and a single ipsilateral regional lymph node, concordant with clinical staging of T2N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition conventional staging, Stage III; Stage I by HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma staging criteria).

PET-CT (18F-FDG) WHOLE BODY WITH DIAGNOSTIC CT NECK/CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS

INDICATION:
52-year-old female with newly diagnosed squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue, p16 positive (HPV-associated), confirmed on direct laryngoscopy biopsy. Referred for whole-body FDG PET-CT for initial oncologic staging. Known right level II cervical lymphadenopathy on prior MRI neck. Never-smoker. No prior malignancy.

TECHNIQUE:
Following a fasting period of greater than 6 hours and confirmation of blood glucose at 98 mg/dL, the patient received 14.2 mCi (525 MBq) of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose administered intravenously via peripheral IV access in the left antecubital fossa. Uptake period of 63 minutes. Whole-body PET imaging was performed from vertex of skull to mid-thighs using a 3D time-of-flight acquisition protocol. Concurrent diagnostic-quality CT was acquired with low-dose technique for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. Dedicated contrast-enhanced CT of the neck, chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with IV iohexol contrast (90 mL, injected at 3 mL/sec) following standard pre-medication with no adverse reactions. Images were co-registered and reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. SUVmax values are reported using body weight-based normalization.

COMPARISON:
MRI neck with and without contrast (same encounter, reviewed in conjunction).

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK - PRIMARY SITE:
There is a conspicuously FDG-avid soft tissue mass centered at the right base of tongue, measuring approximately 3.2 x 2.8 cm in its greatest axial dimensions on co-registered CT. The lesion demonstrates markedly elevated glucose metabolism with an SUVmax of 14.2, well above the expected physiologic activity of the tongue musculature and clearly pathologic in character. On the corresponding CT images, the mass appears as a lobulated, moderately hyperdense soft tissue lesion arising from the right posterior tongue base with slight asymmetric fullness extending toward the right glossotonsillar sulcus. There is no CT evidence of cortical erosion of the mandible or adjacent bony structures. The pre-epiglottic and paraglottic spaces appear preserved. The vallecula is not clearly involved. No contralateral extension of the primary lesion is appreciated.

REGIONAL LYMPH NODES:
A single FDG-avid right cervical lymph node is identified at level IIA (jugulodigastric region), immediately deep to the posterior margin of the right submandibular gland and anterior to the sternocleidomastoid muscle. This node measures 2.1 cm in short axis and demonstrates moderately intense FDG uptake with an SUVmax of 9.8. The corresponding CT images show a rounded, partially necrotic lymph node with loss of the normal fatty hilum and peripheral enhancement, appearances entirely consistent with metastatic replacement. No additional FDG-avid or morphologically suspicious nodes are identified in the right neck at levels I, IIB, III, IV, or V. The left cervical nodal chains (levels I through V) are without FDG-avid or pathologically enlarged nodes bilaterally. No retropharyngeal nodal enlargement or abnormal FDG uptake in this region. No parotid, preauricular, or occipital nodal disease identified. No supraclavicular nodal disease on either side.

OROPHARYNX AND UPPER AERODIGESTIVE TRACT:
The nasopharynx, soft palate, uvula, bilateral palatine tonsils, and posterior oropharyngeal wall demonstrate symmetric, physiologic FDG distribution without focal hypermetabolic abnormality. The larynx and hypopharynx appear symmetric with expected midline structures intact; no focal lesion or abnormal metabolic activity. Piriform sinuses are patent and symmetric bilaterally. No tracheal or esophageal abnormality.

SALIVARY GLANDS:
The parotid and submandibular glands demonstrate symmetric and physiologic FDG activity. No focal mass or abnormal uptake.

SKULL BASE AND INTRACRANIAL STRUCTURES:
No FDG-avid skull base or intracranial lesions. The calvarium and visualized facial bones are without lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement. No perineural spread pattern of disease along the expected courses of cranial nerves V or VII.

THYROID GLAND:
The thyroid is symmetric and without focal FDG-avid lesion. Gland volume within normal limits. Known clinical hypothyroidism on levothyroxine therapy.

CHEST:
The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, consolidation, or pleural effusions. The lung parenchyma is diffusely aerated without focal areas of atelectasis or infiltrate that would raise concern for synchronous primary or metastatic disease. No FDG-avid pulmonary lesions. The mediastinal and hilar lymph node chains are without enlargement or FDG-avid disease. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses. Heart size and pericardium appear unremarkable. The aorta and great vessels are within normal caliber for age.

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:
The liver parenchyma is homogeneous and demonstrates uniform background hepatic FDG activity (reference SUVmean 2.4) without focal area of increased or decreased uptake to suggest metastatic involvement. No hepatic cysts or masses on CT. The spleen is not enlarged and demonstrates physiologic splenic FDG activity. The pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys are unremarkable. No abdominal or pelvic lymphadenopathy. The bowel demonstrates expected heterogeneous physiologic activity without a discrete FDG-avid lesion. Urinary bladder contains expected radiotracer in the urine. The uterus and adnexa are not visualized separately given standard PET attenuation technique, but no pelvic masses or FDG-avid pelvic lesions are identified.

SKELETON:
No focal areas of abnormal FDG uptake in the axial or appendicular skeleton suspicious for osseous metastatic disease. No osteolytic or osteoblastic lesions on CT. The cervical spine is intact with no bony invasion by the primary tumor.

SOFT TISSUES:
No subcutaneous or soft tissue FDG-avid deposits.

IMPRESSION:

1. Intensely FDG-avid right base of tongue mass (SUVmax 14.2, 3.2 cm), representing the known HPV-positive squamous cell carcinoma primary. No evidence of extension to the contralateral tongue base, vallecula, pre-epiglottic space, or adjacent bony structures.

2. Single FDG-avid right level IIA cervical lymph node (SUVmax 9.8, 2.1 cm) with CT morphology consistent with metastatic nodal involvement, including central necrosis and loss of normal architecture. No additional nodal disease ipsilateral or contralateral.

3. No contralateral cervical, retropharyngeal, mediastinal, hilar, or supraclavicular nodal disease.

4. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Lungs clear. Liver, spleen, and solid abdominal organs unremarkable. No suspicious skeletal lesions.

5. Overall PET-CT staging findings are concordant with the prior MRI neck and are consistent with locoregionally confined HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue with single right-sided level IIA regional nodal metastasis (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition). Favorable HPV-positive subtype.

SECTION 1: PATHOGENIC / LIKELY PATHOGENIC VARIANTS (CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. PIK3CA c.1633G>A, p.Glu545Lys (E545K)
   Gene Function: Phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha (PIK3CA); encodes the catalytic p110-alpha subunit of PI3K. Activating mutations drive downstream AKT/mTOR signaling.
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tier I (well-established oncogenic driver, known hotspot)
   Variant Type: Missense substitution, exon 9 (helical domain)
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 18.2%
   Coverage at locus: 914x tumor / 289x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: E545K is one of the two most common PIK3CA hotspot mutations (alongside E542K and H1047R). Located in the helical domain, this mutation disrupts an inhibitory interaction between the helical and regulatory domains, resulting in constitutive kinase activation and downstream PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway upregulation. This alteration is well-described in HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC (OPSCC), head and neck SCC broadly, breast, colorectal, and other epithelial malignancies.
   Clinical relevance: PIK3CA E545K is a known activating oncogenic driver. In the context of HPV-positive OPSCC, PIK3CA mutations occur in approximately 20-30% of cases and are associated with PI3K pathway dependency. Alpelisib (a PI3K-alpha selective inhibitor, FDA-approved in PIK3CA-mutant breast cancer) and other PI3K/mTOR pathway inhibitors represent potential therapeutic considerations in the recurrent/metastatic setting. Available clinical trials targeting PIK3CA-mutant HNSCC should be considered if disease recurs.

2. TERT Promoter c.-124C>T (C228T)
   Gene Function: Telomerase reverse transcriptase (TERT); core catalytic subunit of telomerase. Promoter mutations create de novo binding sites for ETS transcription factors, leading to increased TERT expression and telomere maintenance — enabling replicative immortality.
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tier I (well-established oncogenic driver, recurrent promoter hotspot)
   Variant Type: Promoter region mutation (C228T), non-coding
   Allele Frequency (tumor): 22.1%
   Coverage at locus: 987x tumor / 301x normal
   Germline status: Not detected in matched normal (somatic)
   Functional impact: The TERT promoter C228T mutation (also denoted -124C>T) is one of the two canonical TERT promoter hotspot mutations (alongside C250T). These mutations are among the most prevalent somatic mutations across human cancers and are highly recurrent in HNSCC, thyroid carcinoma, glioma, melanoma, and urothelial carcinoma. In HNSCC, TERT promoter mutations co-occur frequently with other alterations driving cell cycle dysregulation.
   Clinical relevance: No direct approved targeted therapy. In HNSCC, TERT promoter mutations have been associated with adverse outcomes in some HPV-negative cohorts but have less established prognostic significance in HPV-positive OPSCC. This alteration is noted for completeness and potential future clinical trial eligibility.

It modulates Wnt/beta-catenin signaling, Hippo pathway activity (YAP/TAZ regulation), and actin cytoskeleton dynamics. Loss-of-function FAT1 mutations disinhibit Wnt and Hippo pathway outputs, promoting invasion and proliferation.

SECTION 4: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS (CNA)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

6. CCND1 Amplification
   Chromosomal Location: 11q13.3
   Estimated Copy Number: 6.4 copies (fold amplification above diploid)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC (amplification of established oncogene)
   Functional impact: CCND1 (Cyclin D1) amplification results in overexpression of cyclin D1, which complexes with CDK4/6 to phosphorylate Rb and drive G1/S cell cycle progression. CCND1 amplification at 11q13 is one of the most prevalent copy number alterations in HNSCC, occurring in approximately 30-40% of cases. In HPV-positive OPSCC, CCND1 amplification can co-occur alongside HPV-mediated p16 upregulation (a paradox of pathway redundancy); however, CCND1 amplification in this context may partially offset the growth-inhibitory effect of p16 overexpression.
   Co-occurring alteration note: CCND1 amplification co-occurring with CDKN2A homozygous deletion (see below) represents a dual mechanism of CDK4/6 pathway hyperactivation — accelerating cell cycle entry via simultaneous cyclin D1 gain and p16 loss. This co-occurrence is biologically consistent and has been described in HNSCC genomic studies.
   Clinical relevance: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) have demonstrated activity in CCND1-amplified solid tumors including HNSCC in early-phase trials. No FDA approval currently exists for CCND1-amplified HNSCC outside of clinical trial settings.

7. CDKN2A Homozygous Deletion
   Chromosomal Location: 9p21.3
   Estimated Copy Number: 0 copies (homozygous deletion)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC (loss of established tumor suppressor)
   Functional impact: CDKN2A encodes p16(INK4A) and p14(ARF). Homozygous deletion results in complete loss of both p16 and p14ARF expression. p16 normally inhibits CDK4/6-mediated phosphorylation of Rb, acting as a cell cycle brake. p14ARF stabilizes p53 by inhibiting MDM2. Complete CDKN2A loss therefore simultaneously dysregulates both the Rb and p53 pathways.
   Interpretive note regarding IHC p16 positivity: It is important to distinguish between p16 protein expression detected by immunohistochemistry (a surrogate marker for transcriptionally active HPV infection) and genomic CDKN2A status. In HPV-associated OPSCC, p16 IHC overexpression reflects HPV E7 oncoprotein-mediated upregulation of p16 transcription (a feedback response to Rb inactivation by E7), not intact p16 protein function. Therefore, p16 IHC positivity can paradoxically coexist with homozygous CDKN2A deletion in a subset of HPV-positive OPSCC cases; the HPV-driven pathway mediates Rb suppression through E7 (rendering p16 feedback irrelevant), while the genomic deletion eliminates any residual p16/p14ARF tumor suppressive function. This interpretation is consistent with published observations in the TCGA HNSCC cohort.
   Clinical relevance: CDKN2A deletion identifies potential sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy (when combined with CCND1 amplification, as in this tumor). Additionally, loss of p14ARF can confer relative p53 pathway impairment. No FDA-approved therapy specifically indicated by CDKN2A deletion in HNSCC.

8. EGFR Copy Number Gain (Low-Level)
   Chromosomal Location: 7p11.2
   Estimated Copy Number: 3.1 copies (low-level polysomy/gain, not high-level amplification)
   Classification: LIKELY PATHOGENIC — LOW-LEVEL GAIN (significance uncertain at this copy number threshold)
   Functional impact: EGFR (Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor) overexpression/copy number gain is among the most prevalent molecular alterations in HNSCC (occurring in up to 80-90% of all HNSCC cases at the protein level). Low-level copy number gain (3-4 copies) is distinguished from true high-level amplification (>6 copies or focal high-level amplification) and is associated with modest receptor overexpression. EGFR is the target of cetuximab (chimeric anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody) approved for HNSCC.
   Note: No EGFR exon 19 (del19) or exon 21 (L858R) activating point mutations were detected. No EGFR exon 20 insertions detected. The copy number gain represents a quantitative alteration only.
   Clinical relevance: Cetuximab is approved for HNSCC, but EGFR copy number alone has not been established as a robust predictive biomarker for cetuximab benefit in HNSCC at the level seen with EGFR-activating mutations in NSCLC. Low-level gain at this threshold does not independently mandate anti-EGFR therapy.

INTEGRATED GENOMIC INTERPRETATION
================================================================================

This HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma demonstrates a molecular profile characteristic of HPV-driven oncogenesis with several co-occurring genomic alterations typical of this tumor subtype.

Oncogenic pathway activation summary:

1. PI3K/AKT/mTOR Pathway (HIGH-LEVEL ACTIVATION):
   PIK3CA E545K activating mutation (somatic, AF 18%) + HPV integration near PIK3CA locus (3q26) represent convergent mechanisms of PI3K pathway hyperactivation. This pathway is among the most commonly altered in HPV-positive OPSCC and represents the primary actionable target identified in this tumor.

2. Cell Cycle / Rb Pathway (HIGH-LEVEL DISRUPTION):
   CCND1 amplification (6.4-fold) + CDKN2A homozygous deletion + HPV E7-mediated Rb sequestration represent triple convergent cell cycle dysregulation. This degree of Rb pathway disruption is notable and may have implications for CDK4/6 inhibitor sensitivity (palbociclib, abemaciclib) in recurrent/metastatic settings.

3. Telomere Maintenance (ACTIVATED):
   TERT promoter C228T mutation enables replicative immortality via telomerase upregulation.

4. Chromatin / Epigenetic Regulation (POTENTIALLY DISRUPTED):
   EP300 missense mutation (VUS) at AF 11% may contribute to altered histone acetylation patterns, though functional significance is uncertain.

5. Notch / Wnt / Cell Adhesion (POTENTIALLY DISRUPTED):
   FAT1 nonsense mutation (Q2401*, likely pathogenic) represents loss of Hippo/Wnt pathway tumor suppressor function.
   NOTCH1 missense mutation (A465T, VUS) may contribute to NOTCH pathway dysregulation, though functional impact is uncertain.

6. HPV Oncogene-Mediated Pathway Suppression:
   HPV-16 E6 protein mediates p53 ubiquitination and degradation → functional p53 loss without TP53 somatic mutation (confirmed: TP53 not mutated).
   HPV-16 E7 protein binds and inactivates Rb → functional Rb loss contributing to cell cycle dysregulation (compounded by CDKN2A deletion and CCND1 amplification as above).

EGFR copy number gain (low-level, 3.1 copies) is a common background finding in HNSCC and does not alter clinical management independently of the primary actionable findings.

The overall molecular landscape — HPV-positive status, absent TP53 mutation, PI3K pathway activation, and Rb/cell cycle pathway disruption — is entirely consistent with published HPV-positive OPSCC genomics (TCGA, ORIEN, HNSCC-specific studies). TMB-Low and MSS status are expected. PD-L1 CPS 12 confirms PD-1 pathway expression.

METHODOLOGY NOTE
================================================================================
This assay interrogates 523 cancer-relevant genes for somatic single nucleotide variants, small insertions/deletions (indels), copy number alterations, and selected structural variants/fusions. Matched normal (peripheral blood) sequencing was performed for germline subtraction. Variants with allele frequencies < 5% were filtered unless supported by > 20 variant-supporting reads. Variants in regions of known technical artifact, segmental duplication, or low mappability were excluded. Copy number calls require confirmation by FISH or additional methods for clinical decision-making in ambiguous cases. This assay is not validated for detection of constitutional mismatch repair deficiency (Lynch syndrome); separate germline MMR testing may be required if clinically indicated. TERT promoter and other non-coding regions are captured by custom probe tiling. HPV integration analysis is performed using alignment of unmapped reads to viral reference sequences with integration junction identification. This laboratory report is for research and clinical use by the treating physician; it does not constitute independent medical advice.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old female, never-smoker, who presents for multidisciplinary evaluation and treatment planning of a newly diagnosed p16-positive squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue with right ipsilateral neck adenopathy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 52-year-old West African-descent woman who works as a high school music teacher. She presented to her primary care physician approximately 3 months prior to this evaluation with a complaint of progressive right-sided throat pain with swallowing and a palpable right-sided neck lump. She had attributed the discomfort initially to a sore throat but noted no improvement over several weeks. She denied any fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss at the time of initial presentation.

She was referred to otolaryngology, where oropharyngoscopy revealed an exophytic, friable, vascular mass arising from the right base of tongue. She underwent direct laryngoscopy with biopsy under general anesthesia. Pathology returned as invasive squamous cell carcinoma, moderately differentiated, with p16 strongly and diffusely positive immunostaining (>75% of cells, block-like nuclear and cytoplasmic pattern), consistent with transcriptionally active HPV infection. Concurrent HPV RNA in situ hybridization confirmed high-risk HPV type 16 expression in tumor cells. Fine needle aspiration of the palpable right level II neck mass was also positive for metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, p16 positive.

Staging workup was completed as follows:

- MRI neck with and without contrast: 3.2 x 2.8 cm enhancing mass at the right base of tongue without extension to the vallecula or pre-epiglottic space; single right level IIA lymph node 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis and peripheral enhancement. No contralateral adenopathy, no retropharyngeal nodes, no bony involvement, no perineural invasion on imaging.

- PET-CT (18F-FDG): FDG-avid right base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2) with single FDG-avid right level IIA node (SUVmax 9.8). No contralateral nodal disease, no mediastinal or hilar nodes, no distant metastatic lesions. Lungs clear.

- Next-generation sequencing (523-gene panel): PIK3CA p.E545K activating mutation (AF 18%); TERT promoter C228T mutation (AF 22%); FAT1 p.Q2401* nonsense mutation (AF 15%); EP300 p.R1627Q missense (AF 11%, VUS); NOTCH1 p.A465T missense (AF 9%, VUS). CCND1 amplification (6.4-fold); CDKN2A homozygous deletion; EGFR low-level copy number gain. No TP53, KRAS, NRAS, or BRAF mutations. No actionable fusions. MSS. TMB 6.2 mut/Mb (low). PD-L1 CPS 12 (SP142). HPV integration detected. A BRCA2 variant of uncertain significance (p.D2723H, AF ~48%) was identified and appears likely germline; germline counseling has been recommended.

Final clinical stage: T2N1M0 — by AJCC 8th edition conventional criteria this is stage III; per the AJCC 8th edition HPV-positive oropharyngeal staging (p16+ pathway), this represents pathologic/clinical stage I, carrying a substantially more favorable prognosis.

The patient now presents today for formal multidisciplinary evaluation and discussion of treatment options. She reports that the odynophagia has worsened somewhat since initial presentation; pain with swallowing is now 5/10 at rest and 7/10 with eating. She is currently managing a soft diet without difficulty maintaining her weight. She has not experienced any aspiration, frank dysphagia to liquids, respiratory symptoms, otalgia, or neurologic symptoms. She reports no new lymphadenopathy elsewhere.

2. Multivitamin daily

4. No anticoagulants, NSAIDs, or herbals of note

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 118/74 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, temperature 36.8°C, weight 76 kg, height 163 cm, BMI 28.6 kg/m².

Head and Neck:
- Scalp: No scalp lesions or lymphadenopathy.
- Face: Symmetric, no facial nerve weakness, no parotid masses.
- Eyes: Extraocular movements intact; no diplopia elicited.
- Ears: Canals clear bilaterally; TMs intact, no middle ear effusion.
- Nose: Patent bilaterally, no epistaxis, no obstruction.
- Oral cavity: Adequate mouth opening (approximately 4.5 cm, three fingerbreadths). Dentition present and in good repair; no dental caries requiring extraction. Tongue: midline, full mobility. Oral mucosa pink and moist, no lesions in the anterior oral cavity. Hard/soft palate: intact, no visible lesion.
- Oropharynx: A firm, exophytic lesion is palpable and partially visible at the right base of tongue, approximately 2-3 cm estimated clinically, with adjacent right tonsillar fossa without gross involvement. Uvula midline. Soft palate elevates symmetrically.
- Neck: Right level IIA — a firm, discrete, approximately 2 cm lymph node palpable in the right submandibular/jugulodigastric region. Non-tender, non-mobile (mildly fixed), no overlying skin changes. No contralateral cervical adenopathy. No bilateral posterior triangle nodes. No supraclavicular adenopathy. No midline neck mass. Thyroid: no palpable nodules, normal size.
- Flexible nasopharyngoscopy (performed in office): nasopharynx clear, no nasopharyngeal mass. Oropharynx: exophytic, friable mass at the right base of tongue, extending toward the right vallecula border but without definitive epiglottic involvement on scope. Extremities: No peripheral edema, no lymphedema of upper extremities.

Skin: No skin lesions. No signs of radiation changes (no prior treatment).

IMAGING (reviewed in detail)

MRI neck (staging): 3.2 x 2.8 cm enhancing right base of tongue mass; single right level IIA node 2.1 x 1.8 cm with central necrosis. No contralateral disease, no distant spread, no mandibular involvement.
